narrative:
"And so, Glaucon, we have arrived at the conclusion that in the perfect State wives and children are to be in common; and that all education and the pursuits of war and peace are also to be common, and the best philosophers and the bravest warriors are to be their kings?"

"That," replied Glaucon, "has been acknowledged."

"Yes," I said; "and we have further acknowledged that the governors, when appointed themselves, will take their soldiers and place them in houses such as we were describing, which are common to all, and contain nothing private, or individual; and about their property, you remember what we agreed?"

"Yes, I remember that no one was to have any of the ordinary possessions of mankind; they were to be warrior athletes and guardians, receiving from the other citizens, in lieu of annual payment, only their maintenance, and they were to take care of themselves and of the whole State."

"True," I said; "and now that this division of our task is concluded, let us find the point at which we digressed, that we may return into the old path."
refashioned:
"So, Glaucon, we've reached the conclusion that in the ideal state, families are shared, education is universal, and the wisest philosophers and bravest soldiers serve as rulers?"

"That's what we agreed," Glaucon replied.

"Yes, and we also established that the rulers would house their soldiers in communal dwellings with no private property. You recall what we said about their possessions?"

"I do -- no one was to own the usual things people accumulate. They were to be warrior-athletes and guardians, supported by the other citizens with nothing more than their upkeep, devoted to their own discipline and the welfare of the whole state."

"Exactly. Now that we've finished that part of our discussion, let's find the point where we went off track and pick up where we left off."
elegant: Socrates recaps the ideal state: communal families, shared education, philosopher-kings, guardian-soldiers without private property, maintained by the citizens they protect. With those foundations established, he proposes returning to the thread of argument they had left behind.
summary: Socrates summarizes the key features of the ideal state they have constructed -- communal living, philosopher-kings, propertyless guardians -- and proposes returning to a previously interrupted line of inquiry.
distilled: Having established the ideal state's foundations, Socrates seeks to resume the interrupted discussion of inferior constitutions.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon. -- Recap of the ideal state from Books III-V: communal families (Book V, 457c-d), philosopher-kings (Book V, 473c-d), guardian class without property (Book III, 416d-417b). -- This passage serves as a transitional bridge back to the classification of constitutions promised earlier but deferred by the long digression on justice, women guardians, and the philosopher-king.

narrative: "There is no difficulty in returning," Glaucon said. "You implied, then as now, that you had finished the description of the State: you said that such a State was good, and that the man was good who answered to it, although, as now appears, you had more excellent things to relate both of State and man. And you said further, that if this was the true form, then the others were false; and of the false forms, you said, as I remember, that there were four principal ones, and that their defects, and the defects of the individuals corresponding to them, were worth examining. When we had seen all the individuals, and finally agreed as to who was the best and who was the worst of them, we were to consider whether the best was not also the happiest, and the worst the most miserable. I asked you what were the four forms of government of which you spoke, and then Polemarchus and Adeimantus put in their word; and you began again, and have found your way to the point at which we have now arrived."
refashioned: "It's easy enough to pick up the thread," Glaucon said. "You had claimed to be finished describing the state. You called it good, and said the person who embodied it was good too -- though it turns out you had much more to say about both. You also said that if this was the true form of government, all others were corruptions of it, and you identified four principal corrupt forms. You proposed examining the defects of each, along with the corresponding character types. Once we'd surveyed them all, we were to determine whether the best person is also the happiest and the worst the most miserable. I asked you to name the four forms, but then Polemarchus and Adeimantus interrupted, and the conversation took a long detour to where we are now."
elegant: Glaucon recalls the interrupted plan: Socrates had identified one true form of government and four corrupt ones. The task was to examine each corrupt form alongside its corresponding character type, ultimately determining whether justice or injustice leads to greater happiness.
summary: Glaucon recounts the original plan of the discussion: having established the ideal state, they were to examine four inferior forms of government and their corresponding personality types to determine whether justice correlates with happiness.
distilled: Glaucon reminds Socrates of the promised investigation into four corrupt constitutions and whether justice produces happiness.
notes: Speakers: Glaucon recounting the earlier discussion. -- References the interruption at Book V, 449b, where Polemarchus and Adeimantus diverted the conversation toward women, children, and philosopher-kings. -- The four-five state scheme: aristocracy (ideal), timocracy, oligarchy, democracy, tyranny. -- The happiness question connects to Book IX's final verdict on the tyrant's misery. -- The state-soul parallel is the central methodological device of the Republic.

narrative:
"Your recollection," I said, "is most exact."

"Then, like a wrestler," he replied, "you must put yourself again in the same position; and let me ask the same questions, and do you give me the same answer which you were about to give me then."

"Yes, if I can, I will," I said.

"I shall particularly wish to hear what were the four constitutions of which you were speaking."
refashioned:
"Your memory is perfect," I said.

"Then like a wrestler returning to his stance," Glaucon replied, "get back into position. Let me ask the same questions, and you give me the answers you were about to give before."

"I'll do my best," I said.

"What I most want to hear about are the four constitutions you mentioned."
elegant: Glaucon, comparing the discussion to a wrestling match, asks Socrates to resume his position and finally name the four inferior constitutions.
summary: Glaucon uses a wrestling metaphor to urge Socrates back to the interrupted argument, pressing him to identify the four defective forms of government.
distilled: Glaucon insists Socrates resume the interrupted analysis of the four inferior constitutions.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon. -- The wrestling metaphor (palaistes) reflects the agonistic culture of Athenian intellectual life. -- Greek philosophical dialogue as competitive sport -- see also Protagoras 339e. -- The four constitutions to be named: timocracy, oligarchy, democracy, tyranny.

narrative:
"That question," I said, "is easily answered: the four governments of which I spoke, so far as they have distinct names, are, first, those of Crete and Sparta, which are generally applauded; what is termed oligarchy comes next; this is not equally approved, and is a form of government which teems with evils: thirdly, democracy, which naturally follows oligarchy, although very different: and lastly comes tyranny, great and famous, which differs from them all, and is the fourth and worst disorder of a State."

"I do not know, do you? of any other constitution which can be said to have a distinct character. There are lordships and principalities which are bought and sold, and some other intermediate forms of government. But these are nondescripts and may be found equally among Hellenes and among barbarians."
refashioned:
"That's easily answered," I said. "The four forms of government I had in mind, at least those with recognized names, are: first, the Cretan and Spartan model, which most people admire; second, oligarchy, which is far less respected and riddled with problems; third, democracy, which naturally emerges from oligarchy despite being very different from it; and fourth and worst, tyranny -- the ultimate disease of the state."

"I can't think of any other constitution with a truly distinct identity. There are hereditary lordships and governments that are essentially bought and sold, along with various hybrid forms. But these are hard to classify and can be found among Greeks and non-Greeks alike."
elegant: Socrates names the four degenerating constitutions: timocracy (the Spartan model), oligarchy, democracy, and tyranny -- each worse than the last. Other governmental forms exist but lack distinct character.
summary: Socrates identifies the four inferior constitutions in descending order: the Spartan-Cretan system (timocracy), oligarchy, democracy, and tyranny, dismissing other governmental forms as hybrid and indistinct.
distilled: The four degenerate constitutions are timocracy, oligarchy, democracy, and tyranny, each representing a progressive decline from the ideal.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon. -- Crete and Sparta: admired by many Greeks for military discipline and stability; see also Plato's Laws, Books I-III. -- The descending sequence of degeneration is central to Book VIII's architecture. -- 'Lordships and principalities' likely refers to Persian-style monarchies and purchased tyrannies. -- Hellenes and barbarians: the Greek/non-Greek distinction. -- Aristotle later expands this taxonomy in Politics Books III-VI.

narrative:
"Yes," he replied, "we certainly hear of many curious forms of government which exist among them."

"Do you know," I said, "that governments vary as the dispositions of men vary, and that there must be as many of the one as there are of the other? For we cannot suppose that States are made of 'oak and rock,' and not out of the human natures which are in them, and which in a figure turn the scale and draw other things after them?"

"Yes," he said, "the States are as the men are; they grow out of human characters."

"Then if the constitutions of States are five, the dispositions of individual minds will also be five?"

"Certainly."

"Him who answers to aristocracy, and whom we rightly call just and good, we have already described."
refashioned:
"Yes," Glaucon replied, "we hear about all sorts of unusual governments among them."

"Do you see," I said, "that forms of government vary exactly as human characters vary? There must be as many types of one as of the other. States aren't made of oak and rock -- they're made of the people in them, whose natures tip the balance and pull everything else along."

"The states mirror the men," he agreed. "They grow out of human character."

"So if there are five types of constitution, there must also be five types of individual soul?"

"Certainly."

"And the person who corresponds to aristocracy -- the one we rightly call just and good -- we've already described."
elegant: States are not made of wood and stone but of human character. Five constitutions correspond to five types of soul. The aristocratic person -- just and good -- has already been described; the four inferior types remain.
summary: Socrates establishes that constitutions mirror the characters of their citizens, so five forms of government correspond to five types of individual soul. The just, aristocratic type has been described; four inferior types await examination.
distilled: Governments grow from human character: five constitutions correspond to five soul-types, of which only the aristocratic has been described.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon. -- 'Oak and rock': an allusion to Homer, Odyssey 19.163, a proverbial expression meaning 'from nowhere' or 'from inanimate origins.' -- The state-soul analogy is the Republic's central structural device, established in Book II (368c-369a). -- Five constitutions: aristocracy, timocracy, oligarchy, democracy, tyranny. -- The aristocratic/just person was described in Book IV (441d-442d).

narrative:
"We have."

"Then let us now proceed to describe the inferior sort of natures, being the contentious and ambitious, who answer to the Spartan polity; also the oligarchical, democratical, and tyrannical. Let us place the most just by the side of the most unjust, and when we see them we shall be able to compare the relative happiness or unhappiness of him who leads a life of pure justice or pure injustice. The enquiry will then be completed. And we shall know whether we ought to pursue injustice, as Thrasymachus advises, or in accordance with the conclusions of the argument to prefer justice."
refashioned:
"We have."

"Then let's move on to the inferior character types: the competitive and ambitious person who corresponds to the Spartan system, then the oligarchic, democratic, and tyrannical types. By placing the most just person beside the most unjust, we can compare their relative happiness and misery. That will complete our investigation. And we'll finally know whether we should pursue injustice, as Thrasymachus urged, or follow the argument and choose justice."
elegant: The plan: describe the four inferior character types -- timocratic, oligarchic, democratic, tyrannical -- and compare the most just with the most unjust to settle whether justice or injustice leads to happiness.
summary: Socrates outlines the plan to examine four inferior character types alongside their constitutions, ultimately comparing the most just person with the most unjust to determine which life is happier -- resolving the challenge Thrasymachus posed in Book I.
distilled: The investigation will compare just and unjust lives to determine whether justice or injustice produces happiness.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon. -- Thrasymachus' challenge from Book I (338c-339a): justice is the advantage of the stronger, and injustice is more profitable. -- This frames the entire Republic: the answer comes in Book IX (580a-588a). -- 'Spartan polity': timocracy, the honor-loving constitution. -- The comparison of most just (philosopher-king) vs. most unjust (tyrant) is the Republic's dramatic climax.

narrative:
"Certainly," he replied, "we must do as you say."

"Shall we follow our old plan, which we adopted with a view to clearness, of taking the State first and then proceeding to the individual, and begin with the government of honour? -- I know of no name for such a government other than timocracy, or perhaps timarchy. We will compare with this the like character in the individual; and, after that, consider oligarchical man; and then again we will turn our attention to democracy and the democratical man; and lastly, we will go and view the city of tyranny, and once more take a look into the tyrant's soul, and try to arrive at a satisfactory decision."

"That way of viewing and judging of the matter will be very suitable."

"First, then," I said, "let us enquire how timocracy (the government of honour) arises out of aristocracy (the government of the best). Clearly, all political changes originate in divisions of the actual governing power; a government which is united, however small, cannot be moved."
refashioned:
"Certainly," he replied, "we should do as you say."

"Shall we follow our established method -- examining the state first, then the corresponding individual? We'll begin with the government based on honor. I don't know any name for it other than timocracy, or perhaps timarchy. We'll compare that constitution with its matching character type, then move on to the oligarchic person, then the democratic person, and finally we'll examine the city under tyranny and peer into the tyrant's soul, aiming for a definitive conclusion."

"That approach sounds perfectly suited to the task."

"First, then," I said, "let's ask how timocracy -- the government of honor -- arises from aristocracy -- the government of the best. One principle is clear: all political revolutions originate in divisions within the ruling class itself. A government that remains united, however small, cannot be overthrown."
elegant: Following the method of examining each state before its corresponding individual, Socrates begins with timocracy. His foundational principle: all political change originates in divisions within the ruling class. A united government, however small, is unassailable.
summary: Socrates proposes examining each constitution and its corresponding soul type in order, starting with timocracy. He establishes the key principle that political revolution always begins with division within the ruling class -- a united government cannot be overthrown.
distilled: Political decay begins when the ruling class divides against itself; a united government, however small, cannot be moved.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon. -- Timocracy (from time, 'honor'): the government of honor, modeled on Sparta and Crete. -- The method of state-before-individual was established in Book II (368d-369a). -- The principle that revolution requires internal division anticipates Aristotle's analysis of stasis in Politics Book V. -- This passage initiates the descending sequence: aristocracy -> timocracy -> oligarchy -> democracy -> tyranny.

narrative:
"Very true," he said.

"In what way, then, will our city be moved, and in what manner the two classes of auxiliaries and rulers disagree among themselves or with one another? Shall we, after the manner of Homer, pray the Muses to tell us 'how discord first arose'? Shall we imagine them in solemn mockery, to play and jest with us as if we were children, and to address us in a lofty tragic vein, making believe to be in earnest?"
refashioned:
"Very true," he said.

"So how will our city be destabilized? How will the auxiliaries and rulers come into conflict with each other? Should we, like Homer, invoke the Muses to tell us 'how discord first arose'? We could imagine them speaking to us in mock-solemn tones, playing with us as though we were children, adopting a grand tragic style while only pretending to be serious."
elegant: How does the ideal state begin to fracture? Socrates playfully invokes the Muses in Homeric fashion, asking them to explain in mock-tragic tones how discord first entered the perfect city.
summary: Socrates asks how the ideal state could be destabilized and its ruling classes brought into conflict, humorously invoking the Muses in Homer's style to explain the origin of discord.
distilled: Socrates playfully invokes the Muses to explain how discord first arises in the ideal state.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon. -- 'How discord first arose': echoes Homer, Iliad 1.6 (the wrath of Achilles) and the convention of invoking the Muse. -- The 'solemn mockery' signals that what follows (the Allegory of the Allegory and the Allegory of the Allegory) is partly playful, partly serious -- a characteristic Platonic move. -- Auxiliaries and rulers: the two upper classes of the ideal state from Books III-IV.

narrative:
"How would they address us?"

"After this manner: -- 'A city which is thus constituted can hardly be shaken; but, seeing that everything which has a beginning has also an end, even a constitution such as yours will not last for ever, but will in time be dissolved. And this is the dissolution: -- In plants that grow in the earth, as well as in animals that move on the earth's surface, fertility and sterility of soul and body occur when the circumferences of the circles of each are completed, which in short-lived existences pass over a short space, and in long-lived ones over a long space.'"
refashioned:
"How would they address us?"

"Something like this: 'A city built on these principles is nearly unshakeable. But everything that has a beginning also has an end, and even your constitution will eventually be dissolved. Here is how the dissolution works: in both plants and animals, cycles of fertility and barrenness in body and soul follow their own rhythms -- short cycles for short-lived beings, long cycles for long-lived ones.'"
elegant: The Muses declare: even the ideal state must eventually fall, because everything with a beginning has an end. All living things follow cycles of fertility and decline -- and so do constitutions.
summary: Speaking as the Muses, Socrates declares that even the most perfect state must eventually dissolve, since everything with a beginning has an end. Natural cycles of fertility and decline govern all living things, including political orders.
distilled: Everything that begins must end; even the ideal state is subject to natural cycles of decline.
notes: Speakers: Socrates speaking as the Muses. -- The principle that everything generated must perish echoes Heraclitean flux and anticipates the Timaeus cosmology. -- The 'circles' refer to cosmic cycles governing generation -- a Pythagorean and pre-Socratic concept. -- This passage introduces the famous 'Allegory of the allegory' or Speech of the Muses, which leads into the notoriously obscure Allegory Number (8.10).

narrative:
"But to the knowledge of human fecundity and sterility all the wisdom and education of your rulers will not attain; the laws which regulate them will not be discovered by an intelligence which is alloyed with sense, but will escape them, and they will bring children into the world when they ought not.

Now that which is of divine birth has a period which is contained in a perfect number, but the period of human birth is comprehended in a number in which first increments by involution and evolution (or squared and cubed) obtaining three intervals and four terms of like and unlike, waxing and waning numbers, make all the terms commensurable and agreeable to one another.

The base of these (3) with a third added (4) when combined with five (20) and raised to the third power furnishes two harmonies; the first a square which is a hundred times as great (400 = 4 X 100), and the other a figure having one side equal to the former, but oblong, consisting of a hundred numbers squared upon rational diameters of a square (i.e. omitting fractions), the side of which is five (7 X 7 = 49 X 100 = 4900), each of them being less by one (than the perfect square which includes the fractions, sc. 50) or less by two perfect squares of irrational diameters (of a square the side of which is five = 50 + 50 = 100); and a hundred cubes of three (27 X 100 = 2700 + 4900 + 400 = 8000)."
refashioned:
"But your rulers, for all their wisdom and education, will never fully grasp the laws governing human fertility and sterility. Intelligence mixed with mere sense perception will miss these patterns, and they will produce children at the wrong times.

Divine births follow a cycle contained in a perfect number, but human births are governed by a more complex number involving squares and cubes, three intervals and four terms, ratios of similarity and difference, growth and decline -- all made commensurable with one another.

The base number (3), increased by a third (giving 4), combined with five (giving 20), and raised to the third power, produces two harmonies: one a square one hundred times as great (400), the other an oblong figure with one side equal to the first, consisting of one hundred numbers squared on the rational diameters of a square whose side is five (49 times 100 equals 4,900) -- each being less by one than the perfect square including fractions, or less by 100 when using irrational diameters -- plus one hundred cubes of three (2,700). Together: 4,900 plus 2,700 plus 400 equals 8,000."
elegant: Even the wisest rulers cannot master the mathematics of human fertility. They will inevitably breed at the wrong times. The 'nuptial number' -- a complex calculation involving squares, cubes, and harmonic ratios totaling 8,000 -- governs the cycles of generational quality. When rulers fail to align births with this cycle, decline begins.
summary: The Muses explain that even the best rulers will fail to discover the mathematical laws governing optimal human reproduction. The infamous 'nuptial number' -- an elaborate calculation yielding 8,000 -- determines when children of quality will be born. Ignorance of this number triggers the state's decline.
distilled: The rulers' inability to calculate the 'nuptial number' governing optimal births is the first cause of the ideal state's degeneration.
notes: Speakers: Socrates, continuing as the Muses. -- The 'Allegory Number' or 'Allegory Nuptial Number' (546b-c) is one of the most debated passages in all of Plato. Ancient and modern commentators disagree on the exact calculation. -- The number 8,000 (= 20 cubed) is one common interpretation. -- 'Intelligence alloyed with sense': rulers who are not pure philosophers but rely partly on empirical observation. -- The passage draws on Pythagorean number theory and musical harmony. -- Hesiod's ages of gold, silver, bronze, and iron (Works and Days 109-201) are referenced in 8.11.

narrative:
"Now this number represents a geometrical figure which has control over the good and evil of births. For when your guardians are ignorant of the law of births, and unite bride and bridegroom out of season, the children will not be goodly or fortunate. And though only the best of them will be appointed by their predecessors, still they will be unworthy to hold their fathers' places, and when they come into power as guardians, they will soon be found to fall in taking care of us, the Muses, first by under-valuing music; which neglect will soon extend to gymnastic; and hence the young men of your State will be less cultivated.

In the succeeding generation rulers will be appointed who have lost the guardian power of testing the metal of your different races, which, like Hesiod's, are of gold and silver and brass and iron. And so iron will be mingled with silver, and brass with gold, and hence there will arise dissimilarity and inequality and irregularity, which always and in all places are causes of hatred and war. This the Muses affirm to be the stock from which discord has sprung, wherever arising; and this is their answer to us."

"Yes, and we may assume that they answer truly."

"Why, yes," I said, "of course they answer truly; how can the Muses speak falsely?"
refashioned:
"This number governs the quality of births. When your guardians don't understand the law of births and pair couples at the wrong times, the children born will be neither gifted nor fortunate. Even if only the best among them are chosen as successors, they'll prove unworthy of their predecessors. When these inferior guardians take power, they'll begin neglecting the Muses -- first devaluing intellectual and artistic education, then letting physical training deteriorate as well. The young will grow less cultivated with each generation.

Eventually, rulers will emerge who have lost the ability to discern the different metals in human nature -- gold, silver, bronze, and iron, as Hesiod described them. Iron will be mixed with silver, bronze with gold, producing inequality, inconsistency, and disharmony -- which are always and everywhere the seeds of hatred and war. This, the Muses declare, is the origin of all discord."

"We can assume they're telling the truth."

"Of course they are," I said. "How could the Muses lie?"
elegant: Ill-timed births produce unworthy guardians who neglect education -- first the arts, then physical training. Over generations, rulers lose the ability to distinguish the natural metals (gold, silver, bronze, iron) in human souls. The metals mix. Inequality and disharmony follow, breeding hatred and war. This is the origin of all political discord.
summary: When guardians fail to time births correctly, each generation of rulers grows less capable. They neglect education, lose the ability to distinguish natural aptitudes among citizens, and allow the mixing of superior and inferior natures -- producing the inequality and discord that destroy the ideal state.
distilled: Decline begins when inferior guardians neglect education and lose the ability to sort human natures, mixing gold with iron and breeding discord.
notes: Speakers: Socrates (as the Muses) and Glaucon. -- Hesiod's metallic ages: Works and Days 109-201 (gold, silver, bronze, iron races). -- The 'allegory of the metals' was introduced in Book III (415a-c) as the 'noble lie' or 'myth of the metals.' -- Music (mousike) in the Greek sense encompasses all arts presided over by the Muses, including poetry, philosophy, and what we call 'liberal education.' -- Gymnastic (gymnastike) refers to physical and military training. -- The neglect of mousike before gymnastike signals the shift from wisdom-governance to honor-governance (timocracy).

narrative:
"And what do the Muses say next?"

"When discord arose, then the two races were drawn different ways: the iron and brass fell to acquiring money and land and houses and gold and silver; but the gold and silver races, not wanting money but having the true riches in their own nature, inclined towards virtue and the ancient order of things. There was a battle between them, and at last they agreed to distribute their land and houses among individual owners; and they enslaved their friends and maintainers, whom they had formerly protected in the condition of freemen, and made of them subjects and servants; and they themselves were engaged in war and in keeping a watch against them."
refashioned:
"And what do the Muses say next?"

"When discord set in, the two groups pulled in opposite directions. The iron and bronze natures turned to acquiring money, land, houses, gold, and silver. But the gold and silver natures, who didn't want money because they already possessed true wealth within themselves, inclined toward virtue and the old order. A conflict broke out between them, and eventually they compromised: they divided the land and houses into private property. Then they enslaved the very people they had previously protected as free citizens, reducing them to subjects and servants. From then on, the rulers occupied themselves with war and with guarding against the people they had subjugated."
elegant: Discord splits the ruling class: the baser natures pursue wealth while the nobler ones cling to virtue. They compromise by introducing private property -- a fatal step. The former protectors enslave those they once guarded, becoming warriors and overseers rather than philosophers.
summary: When discord divides the ruling class, the baser natures pursue material wealth while the nobler ones defend virtue. They compromise by introducing private property and enslaving the citizens they once protected, transforming the state from an aristocracy into a militaristic regime.
distilled: The ideal state falls when its rulers divide between virtue and greed, compromise by privatizing property, and enslave those they once protected.
notes: Speakers: Socrates (as the Muses). -- The introduction of private property marks the decisive break from the communal ideal of Books III-V. -- 'Enslaved their friends and maintainers': the producer class, formerly free citizens, becomes a subject population -- paralleling Spartan helotry. -- This passage describes the birth of timocracy: a warrior-aristocracy concerned with military dominance rather than philosophical wisdom. -- Compare Rousseau's Discourse on Inequality, which echoes this narrative of property as the origin of social division.

narrative:
"I believe that you have rightly conceived the origin of the change."

"And the new government which thus arises will be of a form intermediate between oligarchy and aristocracy?"

"Very true."

"Such will be the change, and after the change has been made, how will they proceed? Clearly, the new State, being in a mean between oligarchy and the perfect State, will partly follow one and partly the other, and will also have some peculiarities."

"True," he said.

"In the honour given to rulers, in the abstinence of the warrior class from agriculture, handicrafts, and trade in general, in the institution of common meals, and in the attention paid to gymnastics and military training -- in all these respects this State will resemble the former."

"True."

"But in the fear of admitting philosophers to power, because they are no longer to be had simple and earnest, but are made up of mixed elements; and in turning from them to passionate and less complex characters, who are by nature fitted for war rather than peace; and in the value set by them upon military stratagems and contrivances, and in the waging of everlasting wars -- this State will be for the most part peculiar."

"Yes."

"Yes," I said; "and men of this stamp will be covetous of money, like those who live in oligarchies; they will have a fierce secret longing after gold and silver, which they will hoard in dark places, having magazines and treasuries of their own for the deposit and concealment of them; also castles which are just nests for their eggs, and in which they will spend large sums on their wives, or on any others whom they please."
refashioned:
"I think you've correctly identified the origin of the change."

"And the resulting government will be somewhere between oligarchy and aristocracy?"

"Exactly."

"After this transformation, how will they govern? Since the new state occupies a middle ground, it will borrow from both the ideal state and oligarchy, with some features unique to itself."

"True," he said.

"It will resemble the aristocracy in honoring its rulers, keeping the warrior class separate from farming and commerce, maintaining communal meals, and emphasizing physical and military training."

"True."

"But it will differ in its fear of placing philosophers in power -- because genuine philosophers are no longer available, only people of mixed and compromised character. Instead, it will elevate passionate, simpler personalities suited for war rather than peace. It will prize military cunning and wage perpetual wars."

"Yes."

"And men of this type," I said, "will secretly crave money, like those in oligarchies. They'll have a fierce hidden lust for gold and silver, which they'll hoard in vaults and private treasuries, hidden away in fortified estates -- little nests for their wealth -- where they'll lavish money on their wives or anyone else they fancy."
elegant: Timocracy is a hybrid: it preserves aristocratic features like communal meals, warrior separation from commerce, and military training. But it replaces philosophers with warriors, prizes military cunning over wisdom, and wages perpetual war. Beneath the surface, its rulers secretly covet wealth -- hoarding gold in private vaults while publicly despising commerce.
summary: The timocratic state blends aristocratic and oligarchic features: it maintains communal institutions and military training but excludes philosophers from power, replacing them with warrior-types. Its rulers secretly lust after wealth, hoarding gold in hidden treasuries while publicly disdaining commerce.
distilled: Timocracy preserves aristocratic military structure but replaces wisdom with warrior spirit and conceals a growing appetite for wealth.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon. -- Common meals (syssitia): a hallmark of Spartan and Cretan society, also prescribed in Plato's Laws (780d-781d). -- The exclusion of philosophers marks the key difference from aristocracy: honor replaces wisdom as the ruling principle. -- 'Fierce secret longing after gold': the critical flaw that will eventually transform timocracy into oligarchy. -- The private hoarding of wealth despite public prohibition mirrors Spartan hypocrisy -- Spartans were officially forbidden from possessing gold and silver but notoriously accumulated it in secret. -- Plutarch, Life of Lycurgus 9-10, describes similar Spartan contradictions.

narrative:
"That is most true," he said.

"And they are miserly because they have no means of openly acquiring the money which they prize; they will spend that which is another man's on the gratification of their desires, stealing their pleasures and running away like children from the law, their father: they have been schooled not by gentle influences but by force, for they have neglected her who is the true Muse, the companion of reason and philosophy, and have honoured gymnastic more than music."
refashioned:
"Exactly right," he said.

"And they're secretive with money because they have no legitimate way to acquire what they crave. They gratify their desires with other people's money, stealing their pleasures and running from the law like children hiding from a father. They were shaped not by gentle persuasion but by force, because they abandoned the true Muse -- the companion of reason and philosophy -- and valued physical training above intellectual cultivation."
elegant: Timocratic men are miserly in public and indulgent in secret, stealing pleasures like children evading a father. Their character was formed by force rather than reason, because they honored the body over the mind and abandoned philosophy.
summary: Timocratic rulers are secretly greedy, indulging desires with others' money while hiding from the law. Their character was shaped by force rather than persuasion, because they neglected philosophy in favor of physical training.
distilled: Timocratic men are covertly greedy and shaped by force rather than reason, having abandoned philosophy for gymnastics.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon. -- 'The true Muse, companion of reason and philosophy': mousike in its highest sense -- intellectual and artistic cultivation that shapes the soul through persuasion rather than compulsion. -- Law as father: an early instance of the metaphor developed more fully in the Laws. -- The priority of gymnastic over music is the defining educational failure of timocracy. -- Compare Book III (401d-402a) on the role of mousike in forming character through gentle habituation. -- Running from the law 'like children' anticipates the democratic man's childish relationship with desire (8.42-8.46).

narrative:
"Undoubtedly," he said, "the form of government which you describe is a mixture of good and evil."

"Why, there is a mixture," I said; "but one thing, and one thing only, is predominantly seen, -- the spirit of contention and ambition; and these are due to the prevalence of the passionate or spirited element."

"Assuredly," he said.

"Such is the origin and such the character of this State, which has been described in outline only; the more perfect execution was not required, for a sketch is enough to show the type of the most perfectly just and most perfectly unjust; and to go through all the States and all the characters of men, omitting none of them, would be an interminable labour."

"Very true," he replied.

"Now what man answers to this form of government -- how did he come into being, and what is he like?"
refashioned:
"The government you're describing," he said, "is clearly a mixture of good and evil."

"It is a mixture," I said, "but one quality dominates everything else: the spirit of competition and ambition, which comes from the dominance of the passionate, spirited element of the soul."

"Undoubtedly."

"That's the origin and character of this state, sketched in outline only. A more detailed treatment isn't necessary -- a sketch is enough to show the types of the most just and most unjust. To catalog every possible state and character type exhaustively would be an endless task."

"Very true."

"Now, what kind of person corresponds to this form of government? How does he come into being, and what is he like?"
elegant: Timocracy is a mixture of good and evil, but one trait dominates: the spirit of competition and ambition, driven by the spirited element of the soul. With the state sketched, Socrates turns to the timocratic individual.
summary: Socrates concludes that timocracy mixes good and evil qualities but is dominated by competitive ambition rooted in the spirited element of the soul. Having sketched the state, he turns to describe the corresponding individual.
distilled: Timocracy is dominated by competitive ambition -- the spirited element -- and Socrates now turns from the state to the timocratic individual.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Glaucon. -- The 'spirited element' (thumos/thumoeides): the middle part of the tripartite soul from Book IV (439e-441c), associated with anger, honor, and competitive drive. -- In the ideal state, thumos serves reason; in timocracy, it rules. -- 'Sketch is enough': Plato's characteristic methodological modesty about the level of precision appropriate to political philosophy. -- Transition from state to individual follows the established pattern.

narrative:
"I think," said Adeimantus, "that in the spirit of contention which characterises him, he is not unlike our friend Glaucon."

"Perhaps," I said, "he may be like him in that one point; but there are other respects in which he is very different."
refashioned:
"I think," said Adeimantus, "that in his competitive spirit, he's not unlike our friend Glaucon."

"Perhaps in that one respect," I said, "but in many other ways he's quite different."
elegant: Adeimantus playfully compares the timocratic man's competitive spirit to Glaucon's. Socrates concedes the resemblance but insists the differences are greater.
summary: Adeimantus humorously suggests that the timocratic man's competitive nature resembles Glaucon's. Socrates agrees on that one point but notes they differ in many other respects.
distilled: Adeimantus jokes that the competitive timocratic man resembles Glaucon, but Socrates notes deeper differences.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus (who replaces Glaucon as interlocutor here). -- This is a rare moment of personal humor in the dialogue. Glaucon was characterized as spirited and competitive in Book II (357a) when he insisted on a stronger defense of justice than Socrates initially offered. -- Adeimantus takes over as the primary respondent for the remainder of Book VIII, a shift that occurs without explanation. -- The joke works because Glaucon is present and can hear the comparison.

narrative:
"In what respects?"

"He should have more of self-assertion and be less cultivated, and yet a friend of culture; and he should be a good listener, but no speaker. Such a person is apt to be rough with slaves, unlike the educated man, who is too proud for that; and he will also be courteous to freemen, and remarkably obedient to authority; he is a lover of power and a lover of honour; claiming to be a ruler, not because he is eloquent, or on any ground of that sort, but because he is a soldier and has performed feats of arms; he is also a lover of gymnastic exercises and of the chase."
refashioned:
"In what respects?"

"The timocratic man should be more assertive and less refined, yet still appreciate culture. He's a good listener but not much of a speaker. He tends to be harsh with slaves -- unlike the truly educated person, who is above such behavior -- yet he's polite to free citizens and remarkably obedient to authority. He loves power and honor, and claims the right to rule not on the basis of eloquence or intellectual merit, but because he's a soldier who has proven himself in battle. He's also devoted to athletics and hunting."
elegant: The timocratic man: assertive but uncultivated, a listener not a speaker, harsh with slaves but respectful of authority. He claims power through military achievement, not intellectual merit. He loves athletics and hunting.
summary: The timocratic individual is self-assertive but lacks refinement. He appreciates culture without being cultivated, is harsh with inferiors but obedient to authority, and claims leadership through military deeds rather than eloquence or learning. He loves athletics and hunting.
distilled: The timocratic man rules by military achievement rather than intellect -- assertive, obedient to authority, devoted to athletics and combat.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The portrait closely matches the Spartan ideal: laconic speech (good listener, no speaker), military valor as the basis of authority, love of hunting and athletics. -- 'Rough with slaves': contrasts with the philosopher-ruler's temperance; anticipates the discussion of how each regime treats its lower classes. -- 'Too proud for that': the aristocratic man's refinement prevents cruelty, not mere obedience. -- The love of the chase (hunting) was a hallmark of aristocratic and Spartan education; see Xenophon's Cynegeticus.

narrative:
"Yes, that is the type of character which answers to timocracy."

"Such an one will despise riches only when he is young; but as he gets older he will be more and more attracted to them, because he has a piece of the avaricious nature in him, and is not singleminded towards virtue, having lost his best guardian."

"Who was that?" said Adeimantus.

"Philosophy," I said, "tempered with music, who comes and takes her abode in a man, and is the only saviour of his virtue throughout life."

"Good," he said.

"Such," I said, "is the timocratical youth, and he is like the timocratical State."

"Exactly."

"His origin is as follows: -- He is often the young son of a grave father, who dwells in an ill-governed city, of which he declines the honours and offices, and will not go to law, or exert himself in any way, but is ready to waive his rights in order that he may escape trouble."
refashioned:
"Yes, that's exactly the character type that matches timocracy."

"This kind of man will scorn wealth when he's young, but as he ages, he'll be drawn to it more and more. He carries a seed of avarice within him, and he's not single-mindedly devoted to virtue because he has lost his best guardian."

"Who was that?" asked Adeimantus.

"Philosophy," I said, "combined with the arts -- the only force that can protect a person's virtue throughout life."

"A good point," he said.

"So that is the timocratic youth, and he mirrors the timocratic state."

"Exactly."

"Here is how he comes into being: He is typically the young son of a serious, philosophical father who lives in a badly governed city. This father refuses honors and public offices, avoids lawsuits, and doesn't exert himself in civic life. He's willing to give up his rights just to avoid trouble."
elegant: The timocratic man scorns wealth when young but grows avaricious with age, having lost his guardian -- philosophy combined with the arts. He is typically the son of a philosophical father who withdraws from public life in a corrupt city, declining honors and avoiding conflict.
summary: The timocratic man gradually abandons virtue for avarice because he lacks philosophy's protection. He typically originates as the son of a philosophical father who has withdrawn from public life in a corrupt city, refusing offices and avoiding conflict.
distilled: Without philosophy as guardian, the timocratic man drifts from virtue toward greed -- the son of a withdrawn philosopher-father in a corrupt city.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- Philosophy 'tempered with music' (mousike): intellectual cultivation combined with artistic education -- the Republic's ideal formation. -- The father figure represents the aristocratic/philosophical type living under non-ideal conditions. -- His withdrawal from public life mirrors the philosopher's dilemma discussed in Book VI (496c-e): the just man who refuses to participate in corrupt politics. -- The generational transmission of character is a key mechanism in Plato's theory of political change -- each constitution breeds the next through family dynamics.

narrative:
"And how does the son come into being?"

"The character of the son begins to develop when he hears his mother complaining that her husband has no place in the government, of which the consequence is that she has no precedence among other women. Further, when she sees her husband not very eager about money, and instead of battling and railing in the law courts or assembly, taking whatever happens to him quietly; and when she observes that his thoughts always centre in himself, while he treats her with very considerable indifference, she is annoyed, and says to her son that his father is only half a man and far too easy-going: adding all the other complaints about her own ill-treatment which women are so fond of rehearsing."

"Yes," said Adeimantus, "they give us plenty of them, and their complaints are so like themselves."
refashioned:
"And how does the son develop this character?"

"It begins when he hears his mother complaining that her husband holds no position in the government, which means she has no status among other women. She sees him indifferent to money, refusing to fight in the courts or the assembly, quietly accepting whatever comes his way. She notices his thoughts are always turned inward and that he treats her with considerable neglect. Resentful, she tells her son that his father is only half a man, far too passive -- adding all the usual grievances about her mistreatment that women love to recite."

"Yes," said Adeimantus, "they give us plenty of those, and the complaints are very much in character."
elegant: The timocratic son's formation begins at home: his mother, resentful that her philosophical husband holds no office and earns no status, tells the boy his father is weak and passive. The mother's complaints about lost social standing plant the seeds of ambition in the son.
summary: The timocratic character develops when a boy hears his mother complaining about his philosophical father's lack of ambition, social standing, and concern for money. She tells the son his father is only half a man, planting seeds of competitive ambition.
distilled: The timocratic son is shaped by his mother's resentment of his philosophical father's lack of ambition and social status.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The mother's role in corrupting the son is striking: she represents social pressure and conventional values opposed to philosophical withdrawal. -- 'Only half a man' (hemiandr): a pointed insult connecting manliness with public ambition. -- Adeimantus' somewhat dismissive remark about women's complaints reflects Greek gender attitudes but also functions dramatically -- he is Plato's brother and speaks from personal observation. -- The domestic scene as political allegory: the household microcosm mirrors the state's internal tensions. -- Compare Laws 694c-d on the role of women in corrupting Spartan youth.

narrative:
"And you know," I said, "that the old servants also, who are supposed to be attached to the family, from time to time talk privately in the same strain to the son; and if they see any one who owes money to his father, or is wronging him in any way, and he fails to prosecute them, they tell the youth that when he grows up he must retaliate upon people of this sort, and be more of a man than his father.

He has only to walk abroad and he hears and sees the same sort of thing: those who do their own business in the city are called simpletons, and held in no esteem, while the busy-bodies are honoured and applauded.

The result is that the young man, hearing and seeing all these things -- hearing, too, the words of his father, and having a nearer view of his way of life, and making comparisons of him and others -- is drawn opposite ways: while his father is watering and nourishing the rational principle in his soul, the others are encouraging the passionate and appetitive; and he being not originally of a bad nature, but having kept bad company, is at last brought by their joint influence to a middle point, and gives up the kingdom which is within him to the middle principle of contentiousness and passion, and becomes arrogant and ambitious."
refashioned:
"And you know," I said, "that the old household servants also whisper the same things to the boy. When they see someone who owes his father money or wrongs him in some way, and the father does nothing about it, they urge the son to be more of a man when he grows up -- to stand up for himself and fight back.

Whenever the boy goes out into the city, he sees the same pattern: quiet, self-sufficient people are called simpletons and get no respect, while aggressive self-promoters are honored and applauded.

The young man, hearing all this and comparing his father's way of life with what the world rewards, is pulled in opposite directions. His father nurtures the rational element in his soul, while everyone else feeds the spirited and appetitive parts. Since he isn't inherently bad but has been exposed to bad influences, he eventually settles at a middle point: he surrenders inner sovereignty to the spirited element -- contentiousness and passion -- and becomes arrogant and ambitious."
elegant: The boy is pulled between his philosophical father, who cultivates reason, and everyone else -- mother, servants, society -- who encourages spiritedness and appetite. Not inherently bad but surrounded by bad influences, he compromises: reason abdicates, and the spirited element takes the throne. He becomes the competitive, ambitious timocratic man.
summary: The young man is caught between his father's rational influence and the surrounding culture's celebration of aggression and ambition. Servants, peers, and public opinion all urge him to be more competitive. Not naturally bad, he is pulled to a middle point where the spirited element rules his soul, making him arrogant and ambitious.
distilled: Torn between his father's reason and society's celebration of aggression, the young man surrenders his soul's governance to the spirited element and becomes ambitious.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Gives up the kingdom within him to the middle principle': the tripartite soul's internal regime change mirrors the political transition from aristocracy to timocracy. Reason (logistikon) is dethroned; spirit (thumoeides) takes over. -- 'Not originally of a bad nature': Plato emphasizes that corruption comes from environment, not inherent vice -- a key pedagogical principle. -- 'Those who do their own business are called simpletons': ta heautou prattein (minding one's own business) was Socrates' own definition of justice in Book IV (433a-b). The timocratic society inverts this value. -- The servants represent the lower social strata whose values reinforce conventional ambition.

narrative:
"You seem to me to have described his origin perfectly."

"Then we have now," I said, "the second form of government and the second type of character?"

"We have."

"Next, let us look at another man who, as Aeschylus says, 'Is set over against another State'; or rather, as our plan requires, begin with the State."

"By all means."

"I believe that oligarchy follows next in order."

"And what manner of government do you term oligarchy?"

"A government resting on a valuation of property, in which the rich have power and the poor man is deprived of it."

"I understand," he replied.

"Ought I not to begin by describing how the change from timocracy to oligarchy arises?"

"Yes."

"Well," I said, "no eyes are required in order to see how the one passes into the other."

"How?"

"The accumulation of gold in the treasury of private individuals is the ruin of timocracy; they invent illegal modes of expenditure; for what do they or their wives care about the law?"

"Yes, indeed."

"And then one, seeing another grow rich, seeks to rival him, and thus the great mass of the citizens become lovers of money."

"Likely enough."

"And so they grow richer and richer, and the more they think of making a fortune the less they think of virtue; for when riches and virtue are placed together in the scales of the balance, the one always rises as the other falls."

"True."

"And in proportion as riches and rich men are honoured in the State, virtue and the virtuous are dishonoured."

"Clearly."

"And what is honoured is cultivated, and that which has no honour is neglected."

"That is obvious."

"And so at last, instead of loving contention and glory, men become lovers of trade and money; they honour and look up to the rich man, and make a ruler of him, and dishonour the poor man."

"They do so."

"They next proceed to make a law which fixes a sum of money as the qualification of citizenship; the sum is higher in one place and lower in another, as the oligarchy is more or less exclusive; and they allow no one whose property falls below the amount fixed to have any share in the government. These changes in the constitution they effect by force of arms, if intimidation has not already done their work."
refashioned:
"You've described his origin perfectly."

"So we now have the second form of government and the second character type?"

"We do."

"Next, let's examine another man 'set over against another state,' as Aeschylus puts it. But following our method, we'll start with the state."

"By all means."

"Oligarchy comes next, I believe."

"And what is oligarchy exactly?"

"Government based on property qualifications -- the rich rule, and the poor are excluded from power."

"I see," he replied.

"Shall I begin with how timocracy transforms into oligarchy?"

"Yes."

"It's easy enough to see how one becomes the other."

"How?"

"Private accumulation of gold destroys timocracy. The wealthy invent illegal ways to spend their money -- what do they or their wives care about the law?"

"Indeed."

"Then everyone sees their neighbors getting rich and tries to match them. Before long, the whole citizen body becomes obsessed with money."

"That's likely."

"And the richer they get, the less they care about virtue. Wealth and virtue sit on opposite ends of a scale -- when one rises, the other falls."

"True."

"As the state honors wealth and the wealthy, it dishonors virtue and the virtuous."

"Clearly."

"And whatever is honored gets cultivated; whatever is dishonored gets neglected."

"Obviously."

"So instead of loving honor and glory, people become lovers of money. They look up to the rich, make them rulers, and despise the poor."

"They do."

"Then they pass laws setting a minimum property qualification for citizenship -- higher in more exclusive oligarchies, lower in less exclusive ones. Anyone whose property falls below the threshold is shut out of government. They enforce these changes by force of arms if intimidation doesn't do the job."
elegant: Oligarchy grows from timocracy's hidden vice: private gold accumulation. As wealth spreads and envy drives competition, citizens abandon honor for money. Wealth and virtue are inversely related -- what society honors, it cultivates. The timocratic love of glory gives way to worship of riches. Property qualifications replace merit, and the poor are excluded from power by law or by force.
summary: Timocracy degenerates into oligarchy through private wealth accumulation. Competitive greed spreads as citizens imitate one another's riches, and virtue declines as wealth is honored. Eventually, property qualifications are established that exclude the poor from government, enforced by arms if necessary.
distilled: Timocracy becomes oligarchy when private wealth accumulation replaces honor as the ruling principle, and property qualifications exclude the poor from power.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- Aeschylus, Seven Against Thebes 451: 'set over against another state' -- using opposing pairs for comparison. -- 'Riches and virtue on the scales': a famous Platonic image of inverse proportionality. -- Property qualification (timema): historical Athens used the Solonian property classes (pentakosiomedimnoi, hippeis, zeugitai, thetes) to determine political rights. -- 'Force of arms, if intimidation has not already done their work': describes the typical oligarchic coup. -- The transition mechanism is psychological: competitive envy (zelos) transforms collective values from honor to wealth.

narrative:
"Very true."

"And this, speaking generally, is the way in which oligarchy is established."

"Yes," he said; "but what are the characteristics of this form of government, and what are the defects of which we were speaking?"

"First of all," I said, "consider the nature of the qualification. Just think what would happen if pilots were to be chosen according to their property, and a poor man were refused permission to steer, even though he were a better pilot?"

"You mean that they would shipwreck?"

"Yes; and is not this true of the government of anything?"

"I should imagine so."

"Except a city? -- or would you include a city?"

"Nay," he said, "the case of a city is the strongest of all, inasmuch as the rule of a city is the greatest and most difficult of all."

"This, then, will be the first great defect of oligarchy?"

"Clearly."

"And here is another defect which is quite as bad."

"What defect?"

"The inevitable division: such a State is not one, but two States, the one of poor, the other of rich men; and they are living on the same spot and always conspiring against one another."
refashioned:
"Very true."

"And that, broadly speaking, is how oligarchy is established."

"Yes, but what are its defining features and its defects?"

"First, consider the property qualification itself. Imagine if ship captains were chosen by wealth rather than skill, and a poor man were forbidden to steer even though he was the better pilot."

"They'd shipwreck."

"And doesn't the same principle apply to governing anything?"

"I'd think so."

"Including a city? Or especially a city?"

"Especially a city," he said, "since governing a city is the greatest and most difficult task of all."

"So that's the first major defect of oligarchy: selecting leaders by wealth rather than competence."

"Clearly."

"And here's a second defect just as serious."

"What?"

"The inevitable division. An oligarchy is not one state but two: one of the rich and one of the poor, living in the same place and perpetually conspiring against each other."
elegant: Oligarchy's first defect: choosing rulers by wealth instead of competence is like choosing ship captains by property -- a recipe for shipwreck. Its second defect: it inevitably splits the state into two hostile camps, rich and poor, permanently conspiring against each other.
summary: Socrates identifies two fundamental defects of oligarchy: first, selecting rulers by wealth rather than ability, which is as absurd as choosing pilots by property; second, the inevitable division of the state into two hostile classes -- rich and poor -- living side by side in permanent antagonism.
distilled: Oligarchy's fatal flaws are selecting rulers by wealth instead of merit and splitting the state into two warring classes of rich and poor.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The pilot analogy (kubernetes): a favorite Socratic metaphor for expertise-based governance, used in Statesman 297e and elsewhere. -- 'Not one but two states': this analysis of class division anticipates Marx's concept of class conflict, though Plato's remedy (philosophical rule) differs radically. -- Aristotle develops this critique in Politics III.8-9 and V.1. -- The rich-poor division will be the mechanism by which oligarchy degenerates into democracy (the poor revolt).

narrative:
"That, surely, is at least as bad."

"Another discreditable feature is, that, for a like reason, they are incapable of carrying on any war. Either they arm the multitude, and then they are more afraid of them than of the enemy; or, if they do not call them out in the hour of battle, they are oligarchs indeed, few to fight as they are few to rule. And at the same time their fondness for money makes them unwilling to pay taxes."
refashioned:
"That's at least as serious."

"Here's another failing: oligarchies are incapable of waging war effectively. If they arm the masses, they fear their own people more than the enemy. If they don't call up the people, they fight with the same tiny minority that rules -- too few to govern and too few to fight. And their love of money makes them unwilling to pay for any of it."
elegant: Oligarchy cannot wage war: arming the poor majority is dangerous, but fighting with only the rich minority is futile. And their love of money makes them unwilling to fund either option.
summary: Oligarchies cannot conduct war effectively because arming the poor majority threatens the regime, while relying solely on the rich minority provides too few soldiers. Their love of money also makes them reluctant to pay taxes for defense.
distilled: Oligarchy is militarily impotent -- too afraid to arm the poor, too few to fight alone, too miserly to fund a war.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The military weakness of oligarchy contrasts sharply with timocracy's martial strength -- each constitution excels at what the previous one valued. -- The dilemma of arming the poor anticipates the revolutionary mechanism: once the poor realize their military power, they can overthrow the oligarchs. -- Historical parallel: Athenian oligarchs during the coup of 411 BCE and the Thirty Tyrants (404 BCE) faced exactly this problem. -- Aristotle discusses oligarchic military weakness in Politics V.6.

narrative:
"How discreditable!"

"And, as we said before, under such a constitution the same persons have too many callings -- they are husbandmen, tradesmen, warriors, all in one. Does that look well?"

"Anything but well."

"There is another evil which is, perhaps, the greatest of all, and to which this State first begins to be liable."

"What evil?"

"A man may sell all that he has, and another may acquire his property; yet after the sale he may dwell in the city of which he is no longer a part, being neither trader, nor artisan, nor horseman, nor hoplite, but only a poor, helpless creature."

"Yes, that is an evil which also first begins in this State."

"The evil is certainly not prevented there; for oligarchies have both the extremes of great wealth and utter poverty."

"True."

"But think again: In his wealthy days, while he was spending his money, was a man of this sort a whit more good to the State for the purposes of citizenship? Or did he only seem to be a member of the ruling body, although in truth he was neither ruler nor subject, but just a spendthrift?"

"As you say, he seemed to be a ruler, but was only a spendthrift."

"May we not say that this is the drone in the house who is like the drone in the honeycomb, and that the one is the plague of the city as the other is of the hive?"

"Just so, Socrates."

"And God has made the flying drones, Adeimantus, all without stings, whereas of the walking drones he has made some without stings but others have dreadful stings; of the stingless class are those who in their old age end as paupers; of the stingers come all the criminal class, as they are termed."

"Most true," he said.

"Clearly then, whenever you see paupers in a State, somewhere in that neighborhood there are hidden away thieves, and cutpurses and robbers of temples, and all sorts of malefactors."

"Clearly."

"Well," I said, "and in oligarchical States do you not find paupers?"

"Yes," he said; "nearly everybody is a pauper who is not a ruler."
refashioned:
"How shameful!"

"And as we noted, in such a state the same people wear too many hats -- they're farmers, merchants, and soldiers all at once. Does that seem right?"

"Not at all."

"There's another evil, perhaps the worst of all, that first appears in this kind of state."

"What evil?"

"A man can sell everything he owns, and someone else can buy it all. The seller then continues living in a city where he no longer belongs -- he's not a merchant, not a craftsman, not a cavalry officer, not a foot soldier. He's just a poor, helpless nobody."

"Yes, that's an evil that begins here."

"And oligarchy doesn't prevent it -- it produces both extremes of enormous wealth and total destitution."

"True."

"But consider this: even when this man still had money and was spending it freely, was he actually useful to the state as a citizen? Or was he just pretending to be part of the ruling class when in reality he was neither ruler nor subject -- just a spendthrift?"

"He was just a spendthrift, as you say."

"Can we call him a drone -- like the drone in a beehive -- a plague on the city just as the drone is on the hive?"

"Exactly, Socrates."

"Now, nature made the flying drones without stings. But among the walking, human drones, some are stingless and some have terrible stings. The stingless ones end their days as paupers. The ones with stings become the criminal class."

"Very true."

"So whenever you see paupers in a state, you can be sure that somewhere nearby there are thieves, pickpockets, temple robbers, and criminals of every kind."

"Clearly."

"And in oligarchies, do you find paupers?"

"Yes -- nearly everyone who isn't a ruler is a pauper."
elegant: Oligarchy's worst evil: it creates a class of dispossessed citizens who have lost everything but still inhabit the city -- human drones. Some drones are merely helpless paupers (stingless); others become criminals (stingers). Where there is poverty, crime follows. In oligarchies, nearly everyone who isn't a ruler is a pauper.
summary: Oligarchy permits citizens to lose all property and become useless drones in the city. These drones come in two varieties: harmless paupers (stingless) and criminals (with stings). Wherever paupers exist, crime follows. In oligarchies, virtually everyone outside the ruling class is impoverished.
distilled: Oligarchy creates human drones -- dispossessed citizens who become either helpless paupers or dangerous criminals, breeding poverty and crime.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The drone (kephren) metaphor is central to Books VIII-IX: drones represent parasitic, unproductive citizens who destabilize the state. -- Stingless drones = passive poor; stinging drones = active criminals. The metaphor recurs in the analysis of democracy (8.40, 8.53-54). -- 'Cutpurses and robbers of temples': a catalog of Athenian criminal types. -- The specialization principle from Book II (369e-370c) -- each person should do one job -- is violated in oligarchy. -- 'Nearly everybody is a pauper who is not a ruler': this extreme concentration of wealth is what triggers the democratic revolution.

narrative:
"And may we be so bold as to affirm that there are also many criminals to be found in them, rogues who have stings, and whom the authorities are careful to restrain by force?"

"Certainly, we may be so bold."

"The existence of such persons is to be attributed to want of education, ill-training, and an evil constitution of the State?"

"True."

"Such, then, is the form and such are the evils of oligarchy; and there may be many other evils."

"Very likely."

"Then oligarchy, or the form of government in which the rulers are elected for their wealth, may now be dismissed. Let us next proceed to consider the nature and origin of the individual who answers to this State."

"By all means."

"Does not the timocratical man change into the oligarchical on this wise?"
refashioned:
"And can we say with confidence that these states also harbor many criminals -- rogues with stings whom the authorities must restrain by force?"

"We certainly can."

"And the existence of such criminals is due to lack of education, poor upbringing, and a corrupt constitution?"

"True."

"So those are the features and evils of oligarchy, though there are likely many others."

"Very likely."

"Then let's move on from oligarchy -- government by wealth -- and consider the nature and origin of the oligarchic individual."

"By all means."

"Doesn't the timocratic man transform into the oligarchic man in this way?"
elegant: Oligarchy breeds criminals through poor education and corrupt institutions. Having cataloged its evils, Socrates transitions from the oligarchic state to the oligarchic individual, asking how the timocratic man transforms into the money-lover.
summary: Socrates attributes oligarchy's criminal class to lack of education and corrupt institutions. Having surveyed the state's defects, he transitions to examining the oligarchic individual and how he develops from the timocratic type.
distilled: Oligarchy breeds criminals through failed education; Socrates now turns to trace how the timocratic man becomes the oligarchic man.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Want of education, ill-training': Plato consistently traces social ills to educational failure rather than inherent human wickedness. -- The transition from state to individual follows the established methodological pattern. -- The transformation from timocratic to oligarchic man parallels the political transition: both involve the displacement of honor by wealth as the ruling value.

narrative:
"How?"

"A time arrives when the representative of timocracy has a son: at first he begins by emulating his father and walking in his footsteps, but presently he sees him of a sudden foundering against the State as upon a sunken reef, and he and all that he has is lost; he may have been a general or some other high officer who is brought to trial under a prejudice raised by informers, and either put to death, or exiled, or deprived of the privileges of a citizen, and all his property taken from him."
refashioned:
"How?"

"Eventually the timocratic man has a son. At first the boy admires his father and follows in his footsteps. But then he watches his father crash against the state like a ship on a hidden reef -- everything is lost. Perhaps the father was a general or high official who gets dragged into court on charges whipped up by informers. He's put to death, or exiled, or stripped of his citizenship, and all his property is confiscated."
elegant: The oligarchic man begins as the son of a timocratic father who is destroyed by the state -- tried on false charges, exiled or executed, stripped of everything. The son's trauma reshapes his values.
summary: The oligarchic man originates as the son of a timocratic father who is ruined by the state -- a general or official brought down by informers, tried, and stripped of property, citizenship, or life. This traumatic experience transforms the son.
distilled: The oligarchic character is born when a timocratic father is destroyed by the state, teaching his son that honor is dangerous and only wealth provides security.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Foundering as upon a sunken reef': a vivid nautical metaphor for political ruin. -- Informers (sykophantai): a notorious feature of Athenian democratic politics. Professional accusers who brought charges for personal gain were widely despised. -- The trial and ruin of prominent citizens was common in Athens: Themistocles, Cimon, and later Socrates himself all faced prosecution. -- The generational mechanism: each character type is produced by the traumatic failure of the previous type's values.

narrative:
"Nothing more likely."

"And the son has seen and known all this -- he is a ruined man, and his fear has taught him to knock ambition and passion head-foremost from his bosom's throne; humbled by poverty he takes to money-making and by mean and miserly savings and hard work gets a fortune together. Is not such an one likely to seat the concupiscent and covetous element on the vacant throne and to suffer it to play the great king within him, girt with tiara and chain and scimitar?"
refashioned:
"Nothing more likely."

"The son has witnessed all of this -- he's a ruined man. Fear teaches him to overthrow ambition and passion from the throne within his soul. Humbled by poverty, he turns to money-making. Through miserly saving and relentless hard work, he builds a fortune. And isn't it natural that he would then seat the appetitive, covetous element on that empty throne, letting it play the great king inside him, decked out in royal tiara, golden chain, and sword?"
elegant: Traumatized by his father's ruin, the son dethrones ambition and passion from his soul. Humbled by poverty, he devotes himself to money-making through miserly labor. The appetitive element -- greed -- takes the throne, ruling him like an Eastern despot adorned with tiara and scimitar.
summary: The son, traumatized by his father's political destruction, overthrows ambition and spirit from his soul's throne. Driven by fear and poverty, he turns to money-making, and the appetitive, covetous element takes sovereign control of his inner life.
distilled: Fear of his father's fate drives the son to dethrone ambition and enthrone greed as the ruling principle of his soul.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Knock ambition head-foremost from his bosom's throne': a violent metaphor for the internal regime change -- spirit (thumos) is overthrown by appetite (epithumia). -- 'Tiara and chain and scimitar': Persian royal insignia, suggesting that appetite rules as an Oriental despot -- absolute, luxurious, and arbitrary. -- The imagery of the Great King echoes Greek anti-Persian rhetoric and Herodotus. -- This is the second stage of degeneration: aristocracy -> timocracy (reason dethroned by spirit) -> oligarchy (spirit dethroned by appetite for wealth).

narrative:
"Most true," he replied.

"And when he has made reason and spirit sit down on the ground obediently on either side of their sovereign, and taught them to know their place, he compels the one to think only of how lesser sums may be turned into larger ones, and will not allow the other to worship and admire anything but riches and rich men, or to be ambitious of anything so much as the acquisition of wealth and the means of acquiring it."
refashioned:
"Exactly right," he replied.

"Once appetite is enthroned, reason and spirit are made to sit obediently on the ground on either side of their new master. Reason is forced to calculate nothing but how to turn small sums into large ones. Spirit is forbidden to admire or pursue anything except wealth and wealthy people -- its only ambition becomes the acquisition of money and the means to get more."
elegant: Under the oligarchic soul's new regime, reason becomes a mere accountant and spirit a worshipper of wealth. Both are enslaved to appetite, serving only the goal of accumulation.
summary: In the oligarchic soul, reason and spirit are subordinated to the appetitive element. Reason is reduced to calculating profit, and spirit is redirected from honor to admiring wealth and wealthy people -- both serve only the goal of money-making.
distilled: The oligarchic soul enslaves reason as an accountant and spirit as a wealth-worshipper, both serving appetite's drive to accumulate.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The image of reason and spirit sitting on the ground beside the enthroned appetite inverts the ideal arrangement from Book IV (441e-442a), where reason rules with spirit as its ally. -- Reason (logistikon) degraded to mere instrumental calculation: a key Platonic insight about how intelligence can serve base ends. -- Spirit (thumoeides) redirected from honor to wealth-worship: the timocratic love of glory becomes the oligarchic love of money. -- This passage anticipates Aristotle's distinction between practical wisdom (phronesis) and mere cleverness (deinotes) in Nicomachean Ethics VI.12.

narrative:
"Of all changes," he said, "there is none so speedy or so sure as the conversion of the ambitious youth into the avaricious one."

"And the avaricious," I said, "is the oligarchical youth?"

"Yes," he said; "at any rate the individual out of whom he came is like the State out of which oligarchy came."
