"Let us then consider whether there is any likeness between them."

"Very good."

"First, then, they resemble one another in the value which they set upon wealth?"

"Certainly."

"Also in their penurious, laborious character; the individual only satisfies his necessary appetites, and confines his expenditure to them; his other desires he subdues, under the idea that they are unprofitable."

"True."

"He is a shabby fellow, who saves something out of everything and makes a purse for himself; and this is the sort of man whom the vulgar applaud. Is he not a true image of the State which he represents?"

"He appears to me to be so; at any rate money is highly valued by him as well as by the State."

"You see that he is not a man of cultivation," I said.

"I imagine not," he said; "had he been educated he would never have made a blind god director of his chorus, or given him chief honour."

"Excellent!" I said. "Yet consider: Must we not further admit that owing to this want of cultivation there will be found in him dronelike desires as of pauper and rogue, which are forcibly kept down by his general habit of life?"

"True."

"Do you know where you will have to look if you want to discover his rogueries?"

"Where must I look?"

"You should see him where he has some great opportunity of acting dishonestly, as in the guardianship of an orphan."

"Aye."

"It will be clear enough then that in his ordinary dealings which give him a reputation for honesty he coerces his bad passions by an enforced virtue; not making them see that they are wrong, or taming them by reason, but by necessity and fear constraining them, and because he trembles for his possessions."
refashioned:
"Of all transformations," he said, "none is faster or more certain than the conversion of the ambitious youth into the greedy one."

"And the greedy one is the oligarchic youth?"

"Yes -- at least, the man he came from resembles the state that oligarchy came from."

"Then let's see if the oligarchic man resembles the oligarchic state."

"Very good."

"First, they both place supreme value on wealth?"

"Certainly."

"And both are stingy and hard-working. The oligarchic man satisfies only his necessary appetites and restricts his spending to bare essentials. All other desires he suppresses as unprofitable."

"True."

"He's a shabby fellow who pinches pennies on everything and builds up his savings -- and ordinary people actually admire this. Isn't he a perfect image of the state he represents?"

"He seems to be. Money is everything to both him and his state."

"You can see he's not a cultivated man," I said.

"Certainly not," he replied. "If he had real education, he'd never have made a blind god the director of his life's chorus or given him the place of highest honor."

"Well said! But consider: doesn't his lack of cultivation also mean he harbors dronelike desires -- the appetites of a pauper and a rogue -- which he forcibly suppresses through his general habits?"

"True."

"And do you know where to look if you want to see his hidden dishonesty?"

"Where?"

"Watch him when he has a major opportunity to cheat -- like serving as guardian of an orphan."

"Exactly."

"Then you'll see clearly that his everyday reputation for honesty is maintained not by genuine virtue but by force. He doesn't persuade his bad impulses that they're wrong or tame them through reason -- he restrains them through necessity and fear, trembling for his possessions."
elegant: The oligarchic man mirrors his state: obsessed with wealth, stingy, hard-working, suppressing all non-essential desires. But his virtue is enforced, not genuine -- he restrains bad impulses through fear for his property, not through reason or understanding. Give him an opportunity like guardianship of an orphan, and his hidden roguery emerges. He has made Plutus, the blind god of wealth, the director of his life.
summary: The oligarchic individual mirrors the oligarchic state in valuing wealth above all else. He is stingy, hard-working, and suppresses unnecessary desires -- but his apparent virtue is maintained by fear, not reason. When given opportunities for dishonesty, like managing an orphan's estate, his suppressed roguish impulses surface.
distilled: The oligarchic man's virtue is counterfeit -- maintained by fear of losing wealth, not by rational understanding; given the chance, his suppressed desires emerge as dishonesty.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Blind god director of his chorus': Plutus (Wealth) was traditionally depicted as blind -- wealth is distributed without regard to merit. Making him choregus (chorus director, a prestigious civic role) means giving wealth the highest honor. Aristophanes' play Plutus explores similar themes. -- 'Guardianship of an orphan': a proverbial test of honesty in Athens, where orphans' estates were frequently embezzled by guardians. See Demosthenes' speeches against his guardians. -- 'Enforced virtue' vs. genuine virtue: a crucial Platonic distinction. True virtue comes from rational understanding (Book IV, 441e-442b); the oligarchic man's restraint is merely prudential fear. -- The dronelike desires kept forcibly down will eventually break free in the democratic son.

narrative:
"To be sure."

"Yes, indeed, my dear friend, but you will find that the natural desires of the drone commonly exist in him all the same whenever he has to spend what is not his own."

"Yes, and they will be strong in him too."

"The man, then, will be at war with himself; he will be two men, and not one; but, in general, his better desires will be found to prevail over his inferior ones."

"True."

"For these reasons such an one will be more respectable than most people; yet the true virtue of a unanimous and harmonious soul will flee far away and never come near him."
refashioned:
"To be sure."

"But you'll find that whenever he gets to spend other people's money, the drone's natural desires come alive in him."

"And they'll be strong."

"So this man is at war with himself -- he's two men, not one. Generally, his better impulses prevail over the worse ones."

"True."

"For this reason he'll seem more respectable than most people. But the true virtue that comes from a unified, harmonious soul will never come anywhere near him."
elegant: The oligarchic man is internally divided -- respectable on the surface, but at war with himself. His better impulses usually win, but true virtue, which requires a harmonious soul, remains forever beyond his reach.
summary: The oligarchic man is at war with himself -- a divided soul where better impulses usually prevail over worse ones. He appears respectable, but genuine virtue, which requires inner harmony, is permanently out of reach.
distilled: The oligarchic man is internally divided and superficially respectable, but true virtue -- which requires inner harmony -- is beyond him.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Two men, not one': the internal division (stasis) of the oligarchic soul mirrors the class division of the oligarchic state (8.22). -- 'Unanimous and harmonious soul': the just soul described in Book IV (443c-e), where all three parts agree on who should rule. -- 'Better desires prevail over inferior ones': a qualified good -- the oligarchic man is better than the democratic or tyrannical man, but his self-control is prudential rather than principled. -- The theme of inner warfare anticipates the democratic man's complete loss of internal order (8.44-8.46).

narrative: "And surely, the miser individually will be an ignoble competitor in a State for any prize of victory, or other object of honourable ambition; he will not spend his money in the contest for glory; so afraid is he of awakening his expensive appetites and inviting them to help and join in the struggle; in true oligarchical fashion he fights with a small part only of his resources, and the result commonly is that he loses the prize and saves his money."
refashioned: "And the miser will be a poor competitor for any civic prize or honor. He won't spend money pursuing glory -- he's terrified of awakening his expensive desires and recruiting them into the contest. True to his oligarchic nature, he fights with only a fraction of his resources. The usual result? He loses the prize but keeps his money."
elegant: The oligarchic man competes half-heartedly, unwilling to spend on glory for fear of awakening his expensive desires. He fights with a fraction of his resources and typically loses the prize while saving his money.
summary: The oligarchic individual is a poor competitor for public honors because he refuses to spend money in pursuit of glory, deploying only a fraction of his resources. He typically loses the prize but keeps his wealth intact.
distilled: The miser competes half-heartedly and loses the prize but saves his money -- the oligarchic pattern of fighting with minimal resources.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The miser's fear of 'awakening expensive appetites' reveals the precariousness of his self-control: his repressed desires are not tamed, merely suppressed. Any breach threatens total collapse. -- 'Fights with a small part only': mirrors oligarchy's military weakness (8.23) -- too few resources deployed because the rest must be hoarded. -- The parallel between personal stinginess and political weakness reinforces the state-soul analogy. -- This foreshadows the oligarchic man's inability to resist his son's democratic transformation.

narrative:
"Can we any longer doubt, then, that the miser and money-maker answers to the oligarchical State?"

"There can be no doubt."

"Next comes democracy; of this the origin and nature have still to be considered by us; and then we will enquire into the ways of the democratic man, and bring him up for judgement."

"That," he said, "is our method."

"Well," I said, "and how does the change from oligarchy into democracy arise? Is it not on this wise? -- The good at which such a State aims is to become as rich as possible, a desire which is insatiable?"

"What then?"

"The rulers, being aware that their power rests upon their wealth, refuse to curtail by law the extravagance of the spendthrift youth because they gain by their ruin; they take interest from them and buy up their estates and thus increase their own wealth and importance?"

"To be sure."

"There can be no doubt that the love of wealth and the spirit of moderation cannot exist together in citizens of the same State to any considerable extent; one or the other will be disregarded."

"That is tolerably clear."

"And in oligarchical States, from the general spread of carelessness and extravagance, men of good family have often been reduced to beggary?"

"Yes, often."

"And still they remain in the city; there they are, ready to sting and fully armed, and some of them owe money, some have forfeited their citizenship; a third class are in both predicaments; and they hate and conspire against those who have got their property, and against everybody else, and are eager for revolution."

"That is true."

"On the other hand, the men of business, stooping as they walk, and pretending not even to see those whom they have already ruined, insert their sting -- that is, their money -- into some one else who is not on his guard against them, and recover the parent sum many times over multiplied into a family of children: and so they make drone and pauper to abound in the State."

"Yes," he said, "there are plenty of them -- that is certain."
refashioned:
"Can we still doubt that the miser corresponds to the oligarchic state?"

"No doubt at all."

"Next comes democracy. We need to examine its origin and nature, then study the democratic man and bring him before our tribunal."

"That's our method."

"How does oligarchy transform into democracy? Doesn't it work like this? The state's sole aim is maximum wealth -- an insatiable desire."

"What then?"

"The rulers, knowing their power rests on wealth, refuse to pass laws restraining the extravagance of reckless young spendthrifts -- because they profit from their ruin. They charge them interest, buy up their estates, and grow richer and more powerful."

"To be sure."

"The love of wealth and the spirit of moderation can't coexist in the same state. One must be sacrificed to the other."

"That's clear enough."

"And in oligarchies, through widespread carelessness and extravagance, well-born men are regularly reduced to poverty?"

"Often."

"Yet they stay in the city -- stinging drones, fully armed. Some are in debt, some have lost their citizenship, some both. They hate the people who took their property, they hate everyone else, and they hunger for revolution."

"True."

"Meanwhile, the moneyed men walk around hunched over, pretending not to see the people they've ruined. They inject their sting -- their money -- into anyone who lets their guard down, recovering the original investment many times over in compounding interest. And so they fill the state with drones and paupers."

"Yes, there are plenty of them."
elegant: Oligarchy's insatiable greed destroys itself. Rulers profit from the ruin of spendthrift youth, buying their estates and charging interest. The dispossessed remain in the city as armed, embittered drones eager for revolution. Meanwhile, moneylenders create ever more paupers through usurious lending. The state fills with drones until revolution becomes inevitable.
summary: Oligarchy degenerates into democracy through its own greed. Rulers profit from ruining spendthrift citizens through usurious lending, creating a growing class of dispossessed, embittered men who remain in the city, armed and hungry for revolution. The love of wealth and moderation cannot coexist.
distilled: Oligarchy destroys itself as rulers profit from ruining citizens through usury, creating an armed, embittered underclass that eventually revolts into democracy.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The usury mechanism: oligarchs deliberately refuse to regulate lending because they profit from the cycle of debt, dispossession, and estate acquisition. -- 'Insert their sting -- that is, their money': the stinging drone metaphor applied to moneylenders. -- 'Parent sum multiplied into a family of children': interest (tokos in Greek literally means 'offspring') breeds like living things -- a metaphor Aristotle uses in Politics I.10 to condemn usury. -- The dispossessed armed class ready for revolution: this is the immediate cause of democratic revolution. -- Historical parallels: Solon's seisachtheia (debt relief) in Athens c. 594 BCE addressed exactly this crisis.

narrative:
"The evil blazes up like a fire; and they will not extinguish it, either by restricting a man's use of his own property, or by another remedy:"

"What other?"

"One which is the next best, and has the advantage of compelling the citizens to look to their characters: -- Let there be a general rule that every one shall enter into voluntary contracts at his own risk, and there will be less of this scandalous money-making, and the evils of which we were speaking will be greatly lessened in the State."

"Yes, they will be greatly lessened."

"At present the governors, induced by the motives which I have named, treat their subjects badly; while they and their adherents, especially the young men of the governing class, are habituated to lead a life of luxury and idleness both of body and mind; they do nothing, and are incapable of resisting either pleasure or pain."

"Very true."

"They themselves care only for making money, and are as indifferent as the pauper to the cultivation of virtue."
refashioned:
"The evil blazes up like a fire, and the oligarchs won't put it out -- not by restricting what people do with their property, nor by any other remedy."

"What other remedy?"

"The next best option: a rule that all voluntary contracts are entered at the parties' own risk. This would force citizens to evaluate each other's character before lending, which would reduce predatory lending and greatly diminish the state's problems."

"Yes, that would help considerably."

"But as things stand, the rulers treat the people badly for the reasons I described. Meanwhile, they and their followers -- especially the young men of the ruling class -- live lives of luxury and total idleness, both physical and mental. They do nothing and can't resist either pleasure or pain."

"Very true."

"They care about nothing except making money, and they're just as indifferent to cultivating virtue as the paupers are."
elegant: The oligarchic crisis blazes unchecked. A reform requiring that all contracts be entered at the parties' own risk would force character assessment and reduce predatory lending -- but the rulers refuse. They and their idle, luxurious heirs care only about money and are as indifferent to virtue as the paupers they create.
summary: Socrates suggests that requiring all contracts to be entered at the lender's own risk would reduce predatory money-making, but oligarchic rulers refuse all reform. They and their idle heirs live in luxury, incapable of resisting pleasure or pain, indifferent to virtue -- as indifferent as the paupers below them.
distilled: Oligarchy refuses reform while its rulers and heirs grow idle and indifferent to virtue, caring only about money -- mirroring the paupers they create.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The 'caveat emptor' reform: making lenders bear the risk of their own loans would incentivize character assessment and reduce reckless lending. This anticipates modern discussions of moral hazard. -- 'Luxury and idleness both of body and mind': the oligarchic elite's softness makes them vulnerable to overthrow -- they cannot resist pain (military weakness) or pleasure (moral weakness). -- 'As indifferent as the pauper to virtue': rich and poor converge in their lack of virtue, though for different reasons. -- The groundwork for revolution: when the rulers become soft and the dispossessed become numerous and armed, the transition to democracy is imminent.

narrative:
"Yes, quite as indifferent."

"Such is the state of affairs which prevails among them. And often rulers and their subjects may come in one another's way, whether on a pilgrimage or a march, as fellow-soldiers or fellow-sailors; aye, and they may observe the behaviour of each other in the very moment of danger -- for where danger is, there is no fear that the poor will be despised by the rich -- and very likely the wiry sunburnt poor man may be placed in battle at the side of a wealthy one who has never spoilt his complexion and has plenty of superfluous flesh -- when he sees such an one puffing and at his wit's end, how can he avoid drawing the conclusion that men like him are only rich because no one has the courage to despoil them? And when they meet in private will not people be saying to one another 'Our warriors are not good for much'?"

"Yes," he said, "I am quite aware that this is their way of talking."
refashioned:
"Quite as indifferent."

"This is the state of affairs. And sometimes rulers and subjects find themselves side by side -- on a march, a voyage, or a military campaign. In moments of danger, the poor won't be intimidated by the rich. Imagine the lean, sun-hardened poor man standing in battle next to a wealthy, pale, overweight man who has never missed a meal. When the poor man watches the rich man gasping and helpless, how can he avoid concluding that such people are only rich because no one has had the nerve to take their wealth? And when the poor meet privately, won't they say to each other, 'These warriors of ours are worthless'?"

"Yes," he said, "I know very well that's what they say."
elegant: When poor and rich stand side by side in battle, the contrast is devastating. The lean, hardened poor man watches the soft, breathless rich man and realizes: these people are only wealthy because no one has dared to take what they have. The conclusion is inevitable -- revolution becomes thinkable.
summary: When rich and poor serve together in military campaigns, the poor discover the physical weakness of the wealthy. Seeing soft, gasping rich men beside lean, hardened poor men, the poor conclude that the rich are only wealthy because no one has had the courage to overthrow them -- a revolutionary realization.
distilled: Shared military service reveals the rich as physically weak, leading the poor to realize that wealth is maintained only by their own passivity.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The physical contrast between rich and poor soldiers is vivid and psychologically acute: the moment the poor stop fearing the rich, revolution becomes possible. -- 'Wiry sunburnt poor man' vs. the pale, overweight rich man: physical condition as class marker -- laborers are tanned, aristocrats are pale. -- This mechanism of revolutionary consciousness -- the governed discovering the weakness of the governors -- recurs throughout political theory. Compare Machiavelli, Discourses III.6. -- The passage echoes Thersites' challenge to the Greek aristocracy in Iliad II.211-277.

narrative: "And, as in a body which is diseased the addition of a touch from without may bring on illness, and sometimes even when there is no external provocation a commotion may arise within -- in the same way wherever there is weakness in the State there is also likely to be illness, of which the occasions may be very slight, the one party introducing from without their oligarchical, the other their democratical allies, and then the State falls sick, and is at war with herself; and may be at times distracted, even when there is no external cause."
refashioned: "Just as a diseased body can be pushed into illness by the slightest external contact -- or even break down from within with no external trigger at all -- so too a weakened state is vulnerable to crisis from the smallest provocation. One faction brings in oligarchic allies from outside, the other brings in democratic allies, and the state falls into civil war. Sometimes it tears itself apart even without any outside interference."
elegant: A weakened state, like a diseased body, can be tipped into crisis by the slightest touch -- or collapse from within without any external cause. Factions invite outside allies, and civil war erupts.
summary: Like a diseased body that can fall ill from the slightest contact or even from internal disturbance alone, a weakened state is vulnerable to civil war. Opposing factions invite foreign allies -- oligarchic or democratic -- and the state tears itself apart, sometimes without any external provocation.
distilled: A weakened state, like a diseased body, can collapse from the slightest provocation or from purely internal causes, erupting into civil war.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The body-politic analogy: disease of the state as disease of the body is a persistent Platonic metaphor. -- External intervention in civil conflicts: a common pattern in Greek politics. Sparta and Athens routinely supported oligarchic and democratic factions in other cities, respectively. -- The Peloponnesian War (431-404 BCE) was largely fought along these factional lines; see Thucydides III.82-83 on stasis in Corcyra. -- 'Even when there is no external cause': internal contradictions alone can destroy a state -- a key insight of Plato's political theory.

narrative:
"Yes, surely."

"And then democracy comes into being after the poor have conquered their opponents, slaughtering some and banishing some, while to the remainder they give an equal share of freedom and power; and this is the form of government in which the magistrates are commonly elected by lot."

"Yes," he said, "that is the nature of democracy, whether the revolution has been effected by arms, or whether fear has caused the opposite party to withdraw."

"And now what is their manner of life, and what sort of a government have they? for as the government is, such will be the man."

"Clearly," he said.

"In the first place, are they not free; and is not the city full of freedom and frankness -- a man may say and do what he likes?"

"'Tis said so," he replied.

"And where freedom is, the individual is clearly able to order for himself his own life as he pleases?"

"Clearly."

"Then in this kind of State there will be the greatest variety of human natures?"

"There will."

"This, then, seems likely to be the fairest of States, being an embroidered robe which is spangled with every sort of flower. And just as women and children think a variety of colours to be of all things most charming, so there are many men to whom this State, which is spangled with the manners and characters of mankind, will appear to be the fairest of States."

"Yes."

"Yes, my good Sir, and there will be no better in which to look for a government."

"Why?"

"Because of the liberty which reigns there -- they have a complete assortment of constitutions; and he who has a mind to establish a State, as we have been doing, must go to a democracy as he would to a bazaar at which they sell them, and pick out the one that suits him; then, when he has made his choice, he may found his State."
refashioned:
"Yes, surely."

"Democracy comes into being after the poor defeat their opponents -- killing some, banishing others, and giving equal shares of freedom and power to those who remain. In this system, public officials are typically chosen by lottery."

"Yes, that's the nature of democracy, whether the revolution was violent or the ruling class withdrew out of fear."

"What is their way of life, and what kind of government do they have? As the government is, so will the man be."

"Clearly."

"First, are they not free? Isn't the city overflowing with freedom and openness -- everyone saying and doing whatever they like?"

"So they say."

"And where freedom reigns, each individual can arrange his life however he pleases?"

"Clearly."

"Then this state will contain the greatest variety of human types?"

"It will."

"This might seem the most beautiful of states -- like an embroidered robe decorated with every kind of flower. Just as women and children find a riot of colors the most charming thing in the world, many people will think this state, decorated with every type of human character, is the most beautiful of all."

"Yes."

"And there's no better place to go shopping for a constitution."

"Why?"

"Because of the freedom that reigns there -- they have a complete inventory of every kind of government. Anyone who wants to found a state, as we've been doing, should go to a democracy like a bazaar, browse the selection, and pick whichever constitution suits him."
elegant: Democracy is born when the poor overthrow the oligarchs, distributing equal freedom and power and selecting officials by lottery. The result is maximum freedom and variety -- a state like a colorful embroidered robe, containing every type of human nature. It's a bazaar of constitutions: beautiful to behold, but is beauty the same as goodness?
summary: Democracy emerges when the poor defeat the oligarchs and distribute equal freedom and power, with officials chosen by lottery. The result is maximum freedom and diversity -- every human type flourishes. Socrates compares it to a colorful embroidered robe and a bazaar of constitutions, noting its superficial beauty while hinting at deeper problems.
distilled: Democracy arises from the poor's victory over oligarchs, producing maximum freedom and variety -- beautiful like a colorful robe, but beauty is not virtue.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- Election by lot (sortition): the characteristically Athenian democratic practice, as opposed to election by vote (which Aristotle considers oligarchic). -- 'Embroidered robe spangled with flowers': the imagery is deliberately seductive -- democracy's variety is aesthetically appealing but philosophically dangerous. -- 'Women and children' find variety charming: a pointed comparison suggesting that democratic taste is immature. -- The 'bazaar of constitutions': democracy's tolerance contains all possibilities, including its own destruction. -- Socrates' tone is ironic throughout -- he praises democracy's beauty while systematically undermining its claim to goodness.

narrative:
"He will be sure to have patterns enough."

"And there being no necessity," I said, "for you to govern in this State, even if you have the capacity, or to be governed, unless you like, or go to war when the rest go to war, or to be at peace when others are at peace, unless you are so disposed -- there being no necessity also, because some law forbids you to hold office or be a dicast, that you should not hold office or be a dicast, if you have a fancy -- is not this a way of life which for the moment is supremely delightful?"

"For the moment, yes."

"And is not their humanity to the condemned in some cases quite charming? Have you not observed how, in a democracy, many persons, although they have been sentenced to death or exile, just stay where they are and walk about the world -- the gentleman parades like a hero, and nobody sees or cares?"
refashioned:
"He'll have plenty of options to choose from."

"And consider this: in a democracy, there's no obligation to govern even if you're capable, or to be governed if you don't feel like it. You don't have to fight when others fight, or keep the peace when others do. Even if a law bars you from holding office or serving on a jury, you can go ahead and do it anyway if you feel like it. Isn't this way of life, at least for the moment, utterly delightful?"

"For the moment, yes."

"And isn't their leniency toward the condemned rather charming? Haven't you noticed that in a democracy, people who have been sentenced to death or exile simply stay put and walk around freely? The condemned man strolls about like a hero, and nobody notices or cares."
elegant: Democracy is delightful in the moment: no obligation to govern, fight, or obey. Even the condemned walk free -- sentenced to death or exile, they stroll about unbothered. Charming, perhaps -- but what does this leniency reveal about the state's relationship to its own laws?
summary: In a democracy, no one is compelled to govern, be governed, fight, or obey. Even people sentenced to death or exile simply walk about freely, ignored by everyone. Socrates calls this way of life supremely delightful 'for the moment' -- an ironic qualification hinting at its unsustainability.
distilled: Democracy offers delightful freedom from all obligation, where even the condemned walk free -- charming but unsustainable.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'For the moment': Adeimantus' qualification is telling -- democratic pleasure is temporary. -- The condemned walking free: Socrates may be thinking of his own situation -- he was later condemned to death and famously refused to flee, unlike the democratic pattern described here. -- 'Nobody sees or cares': democratic indifference to law enforcement reflects a deeper indifference to justice. -- The passage combines genuine appreciation for democratic freedom with systematic irony about its consequences. -- Dicast (dikastes): a member of the Athenian jury-courts, a key democratic institution.

narrative:
"Yes," he replied, "many and many a one."

"See too," I said, "the forgiving spirit of democracy, and the 'don't care' about trifles, and the disregard which she shows of all the fine principles which we solemnly laid down at the foundation of the city -- as when we said that, except in the case of some rarely gifted nature, there never will be a good man who has not from his childhood been used to play amid things of beauty and make of them a joy and a study -- how grandly does she trample all these fine notions of ours under her feet, never giving a thought to the pursuits which make a statesman, and promoting to honour any one who professes to be the people's friend."
refashioned:
"Yes," he replied, "many of them."

"And notice democracy's forgiving spirit -- her casual 'who cares?' attitude, her complete disregard for all the principles we so carefully established when founding our ideal city. We said that no one could become truly good without being raised from childhood surrounded by beauty, making it both a source of joy and a subject of study. Democracy tramples all these fine notions magnificently underfoot, never considering what training makes a good statesman. She promotes to honor anyone who simply claims to be a friend of the people."
elegant: Democracy cheerfully tramples every principle of careful education and philosophical formation. She doesn't care what training produces a statesman -- she honors anyone who claims to be the people's friend, regardless of qualifications.
summary: Socrates notes democracy's 'forgiving spirit' and casual disregard for the educational principles they established -- that good men must be raised amid beauty and careful study. Democracy ignores what makes a true statesman and promotes anyone who professes to be the people's friend.
distilled: Democracy tramples educational principles and promotes anyone who claims to be the people's friend, regardless of qualifications or character.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Played amid things of beauty': references the educational program of Books II-III (401c-402a), where children are surrounded by beautiful art, music, and environment to shape their souls. -- 'Friend of the people' (demagogos): literally 'leader of the demos,' the term quickly acquired negative connotations of flattery and manipulation. -- Democracy personified as feminine ('she') is characteristic of Greek political discourse. -- The ironic tone is sharp: 'how grandly does she trample' -- Socrates admires democracy's magnificent indifference to philosophy. -- This passage directly connects Book VIII's critique to the educational foundations laid in Books II-III.

narrative:
"Yes, she is of a noble spirit."

"These and other kindred characteristics are proper to democracy, which is a charming form of government, full of variety and disorder, and dispensing a sort of equality to equals and unequals alike."

"We know her well."

"Consider now," I said, "what manner of man the individual is, or rather consider, as in the case of the State, how he comes into being."

"Very good," he said.

"Is not this the way -- he is the son of the miserly and oligarchical father who has trained him in his own habits?"

"Exactly."

"And, like his father, he keeps under by force the pleasures which are of the spending and not of the getting sort, being those which are called unnecessary?"

"Obviously."

"Would you like, for the sake of clearness, to distinguish which are the necessary and which are the unnecessary pleasures?"

"I should."

"Are not necessary pleasures those of which we cannot get rid, and of which the satisfaction is a benefit to us? And they are rightly so, because we are framed by nature to desire both what is beneficial and what is necessary, and cannot help it."

"True."

"We are not wrong therefore in calling them necessary?"

"We are not."

"And the desires of which a man may get rid, if he takes pains from his youth upwards -- of which the presence, moreover, does no good, and in some cases the reverse of good -- shall we not be right in saying that all these are unnecessary?"

"Yes, certainly."

"Suppose we select an example of either kind, in order that we may have a general notion of them?"

"Very good."

"Will not the desire of eating, that is, of simple food and condiments, in so far as they are required for health and strength, be of the necessary class?"

"That is what I should suppose."

"The pleasure of eating is necessary in two ways; it does us good and it is essential to the continuance of life?"

"Yes."

"But the condiments are only necessary in so far as they are good for health?"

"Certainly."

"And the desire which goes beyond this, or more delicate food, or other luxuries, which might generally be got rid of, if controlled and trained in youth, and is hurtful to the body, and hurtful to the soul in the pursuit of wisdom and virtue, may be rightly called unnecessary?"
refashioned:
"Yes, she has a noble spirit indeed."

"These are democracy's defining traits: a charming form of government, full of variety and disorder, distributing a kind of equality to equals and unequals alike."

"We know her well."

"Now let's consider the democratic individual. How does he come into being?"

"Very good."

"He's the son of the miserly, oligarchic father who raised him according to his own habits?"

"Exactly."

"And like his father, he forcibly suppresses the spending pleasures -- the ones called unnecessary?"

"Obviously."

"Should we clarify the distinction between necessary and unnecessary pleasures?"

"We should."

"Necessary pleasures are those we can't eliminate and whose satisfaction actually benefits us. We're built by nature to desire what is both beneficial and necessary."

"True."

"So we're right to call them necessary?"

"We are."

"And desires that can be trained away from youth, whose presence does no good -- or actively does harm -- these are properly called unnecessary?"

"Certainly."

"Let's take an example of each to make this concrete."

"Good idea."

"The desire for simple food, insofar as it sustains health and strength -- that's necessary?"

"I'd say so."

"Eating is necessary in two ways: it benefits us and it's essential for survival."

"Yes."

"And seasoning is necessary only insofar as it contributes to health?"

"Certainly."

"But the desire for elaborate cuisine and luxuries beyond this -- desires that could be eliminated through early training, and that harm both body and soul in the pursuit of wisdom -- these are rightly called unnecessary?"
elegant: Democracy distributes equality to equals and unequals alike -- charming, varied, disordered. The democratic man originates as the oligarchic father's son. To understand him, Socrates distinguishes necessary pleasures (which sustain life and health and cannot be eliminated) from unnecessary ones (which can be trained away and harm both body and soul). Simple food is necessary; luxury cuisine is not.
summary: Socrates characterizes democracy as charming but disordered, distributing equality indiscriminately. Before examining the democratic individual, he draws a crucial distinction between necessary pleasures (those essential for life and health) and unnecessary pleasures (those that can be eliminated through discipline and harm the pursuit of wisdom).
distilled: Democracy gives equality to equals and unequals alike; understanding the democratic man requires distinguishing necessary pleasures from unnecessary ones that harm body and soul.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Equality to equals and unequals alike': a pointed critique -- true justice requires proportional equality (treating unequals unequally), not arithmetic equality. See Aristotle, Politics III.9 and Nicomachean Ethics V.3. -- The necessary/unnecessary pleasure distinction is foundational for the rest of Book VIII and Book IX. Necessary desires: food, shelter, basic needs. Unnecessary desires: luxury, excess, refinement. -- The food example grounds abstract categories in concrete experience. -- 'Hurtful to the soul in the pursuit of wisdom': unnecessary pleasures are not merely bodily indulgences but obstacles to philosophical development. -- This distinction will become the mechanism of the democratic man's formation: his father suppresses all unnecessary desires; the son embraces them.

narrative:
"Very true."

"May we not say that these desires spend, and that the others make money because they conduce to production?"

"Certainly."

"And of the pleasures of love, and all other pleasures, the same holds good?"

"True."

"And the drone of whom we spoke was he who was surfeited in pleasures and desires of this sort, and was the slave of the unnecessary desires, whereas he who was subject to the necessary only was miserly and oligarchical?"

"Very true."

"Again, let us see how the democratical man grows out of the oligarchical: the following, as I suspect, is commonly the process."

"What is the process?"

"When a young man who has been brought up as we were just now describing, in a vulgar and miserly way, has tasted drones' honey and has come to associate with fierce and crafty natures who are able to provide for him all sorts of refinements and varieties of pleasure -- then, as you may imagine, the change will begin of the oligarchical principle within him into the democratical?"

"Inevitably."

"And as in the city like was helping like, and the change was effected by an alliance from without assisting one division of the citizens, so too the young man is changed by a class of desires coming from without to assist the desires within him, that which is akin and alike again helping that which is akin and alike?"

"Certainly."

"And if there be any ally which aids the oligarchical principle within him, whether the influence of a father or of kindred, advising or rebuking him, then there arises in his soul a faction and an opposite faction, and he goes to war with himself."

"It must be so."

"And there are times when the democratical principle gives way to the oligarchical, and some of his desires die, and others are banished; a spirit of reverence enters into the young man's soul and order is restored."

"Yes," he said, "that sometimes happens."

"And then, again, after the old desires have been driven out, fresh ones spring up, which are akin to them, and because he, their father, does not know how to educate them, wax fierce and numerous."
refashioned:
"Very true."

"Can we say that unnecessary desires spend money, while necessary desires actually produce it?"

"Certainly."

"And the same applies to sexual pleasures and all other desires?"

"True."

"So the drone we described was the person enslaved to unnecessary desires, while the miser enslaved only to necessary ones was the oligarchic type?"

"Exactly."

"Now let's trace how the democratic man grows out of the oligarchic one. I believe the process typically goes like this."

"What process?"

"A young man raised in the vulgar, miserly way we described gets his first taste of the drone's honey. He falls in with wild, clever companions who can supply him with every variety of refined pleasure. And so the oligarchic principle within him begins to transform into the democratic."

"Inevitably."

"Just as in the state, where like helps like and outside allies assist one faction against another, so in the young man's soul -- external desires ally with the internal ones that resemble them."

"Certainly."

"And if the oligarchic principle within him has its own allies -- a father's influence, family pressure, advice and rebuke -- then civil war breaks out within his soul."

"It must."

"Sometimes the democratic impulse retreats before the oligarchic one. Some desires die, others are banished, a spirit of reverence enters the young man's soul, and order is restored."

"Yes, that sometimes happens."

"But once the old desires are driven out, new ones spring up -- desires akin to those expelled. And because the father doesn't know how to educate these new desires, they grow fierce and numerous."
elegant: The democratic man emerges when the oligarchic father's son tastes forbidden pleasures and falls in with companions who supply every variety. External desires ally with suppressed internal ones, just as foreign factions ally with domestic rebels. Civil war erupts in the soul. Sometimes order is restored, but new desires keep springing up, and the father -- who knows only suppression, not education -- cannot contain them.
summary: The democratic man develops from the oligarchic father's son who discovers unnecessary pleasures through bad companions. His soul becomes a battleground between oligarchic restraint and democratic desire, with external influences reinforcing internal appetites. Even when order is temporarily restored, new desires spring up that the father cannot educate -- only suppress.
distilled: The democratic soul forms when forbidden pleasures infiltrate the oligarchic son's repressed psyche, sparking an internal civil war that the father's suppressive methods cannot win.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Drones' honey': the pleasures provided by the parasitic drone class -- luxury, refinement, entertainment. -- The parallel between political faction and psychological conflict is explicit: just as cities fall through alliances between internal dissidents and external powers, so souls change through alliances between suppressed desires and external temptations. -- 'Does not know how to educate them': the oligarchic father's fatal flaw is that he suppresses desires by force rather than educating them through reason (compare 8.29). When new desires arise, he has no tools beyond prohibition. -- 'Wax fierce and numerous': biological language suggesting that desires breed and multiply when not properly managed -- anticipating the tyrannical soul's complete enslavement to appetite.

narrative:
"Yes," he said, "that is apt to be the way."

"They draw him to his old associates, and holding secret intercourse with them, breed and multiply in him."

"Very true."

"At length they seize upon the citadel of the young man's soul, which they perceive to be void of all accomplishments and fair pursuits and true words, which make their abode in the minds of men who are dear to the gods, and are their best guardians and sentinels."

"None better."

"False and boastful conceits and phrases mount upwards and take their place."
refashioned:
"Yes, that's exactly how it goes."

"The new desires draw him back to his old companions, and through secret association, they breed and multiply inside him."

"Very true."

"Eventually they seize the citadel of the young man's soul, which they find empty of genuine accomplishments, noble pursuits, and true ideas -- the things that serve as guardians and sentinels in the minds of those who are dear to the gods."

"The best guardians there are."

"In their place, false and boastful ideas ascend and take the throne."
elegant: Desires multiply secretly and seize the citadel of the soul, which they find undefended -- empty of accomplishments, noble pursuits, and true ideas. False and boastful conceits take the place of the absent guardians.
summary: The proliferating desires seize the undefended citadel of the young man's soul, which lacks genuine accomplishments and true ideas -- the natural guardians of the mind. In their absence, false and boastful conceits take control.
distilled: Desires seize the soul's undefended citadel, where false conceits replace the absent guardians of truth and genuine accomplishment.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The military metaphor of the soul's 'citadel' (acropolis) continues the political-psychological parallel: just as a city's acropolis can be seized, so can the governing center of the soul. -- 'Void of accomplishments and fair pursuits': the oligarchic father's failure to provide genuine education (only suppression) leaves the soul undefended. -- 'True words as guardians': logos (rational discourse) as the soul's protective garrison -- a characteristically Platonic idea. -- 'Dear to the gods': those with philosophically formed souls. -- 'False and boastful conceits': the democratic soul's self-deception -- renaming vices as virtues (developed further in 8.42-8.43).

narrative:
"They are certain to do so."

"And so the young man returns into the country of the lotus-eaters, and takes up his dwelling there in the face of all men; and if any help be sent by his friends to the oligarchical part of him, the aforesaid vain conceits shut the gate of the king's fastness; and they will neither allow the embassy itself to enter, nor, if private advisers offer the fatherly counsel of the aged, will they listen to them or receive them. There is a battle and they gain the day, and then modesty, which they call silliness, is ignominiously thrust into exile by them, and temperance, which they nickname unmanliness, is trampled in the mire and cast forth; they persuade men that moderation and orderly expenditure are vulgarity and meanness, and so, by the help of a rabble of evil appetites, they drive them beyond the border."
refashioned:
"They certainly do."

"And so the young man settles in the land of the lotus-eaters, living there openly for all to see. When friends try to send help to the oligarchic part of his soul, those boastful conceits bar the gates of the inner citadel. They refuse to admit the rescue party. If older, wiser advisers offer fatherly counsel, they won't listen or let them in.

A battle follows, and the conceits win. Modesty is renamed 'foolishness' and driven into disgraceful exile. Temperance is rebranded as 'weakness' and trampled in the mud. They convince the young man that moderation and orderly spending are merely 'vulgarity' and 'stinginess,' and with a mob of evil appetites as allies, they drive these virtues beyond the border."
elegant: The young man settles in the land of the lotus-eaters. His soul's citadel bars entry to all counsel. Virtues are renamed as vices: modesty becomes 'foolishness,' temperance becomes 'weakness,' moderation becomes 'vulgarity.' With a mob of appetites as enforcers, genuine virtues are exiled.
summary: The democratic soul enters the land of the lotus-eaters. Wise counsel is rejected, and virtues are systematically renamed: modesty is called foolishness, temperance is called weakness, moderation is called vulgarity. Aided by a mob of appetites, these genuine virtues are driven into exile.
distilled: The democratic soul exiles genuine virtues by renaming them as vices -- modesty becomes foolishness, temperance becomes weakness, moderation becomes vulgarity.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Country of the lotus-eaters': Homer, Odyssey 9.82-104 -- the land where eating lotus fruit makes one forget home and duty, an image of pleasant oblivion. -- The renaming of virtues as vices is one of Plato's most psychologically penetrating observations: moral corruption proceeds through linguistic corruption. Compare Thucydides III.82 on the revolution in language during the Corcyrean civil war: 'reckless audacity came to be considered the courage of a loyal ally.' -- 'King's fastness': the acropolis/citadel metaphor continued. -- 'Fatherly counsel of the aged': traditional wisdom rejected by the democratic temperament. -- This passage anticipates Orwell's analysis of political language in 'Politics and the English Language.'

narrative:
"Yes, with a will."

"And when they have emptied and swept clean the soul of him who is now in their power and who is being initiated by them in great mysteries, the next thing is to bring back to their house insolence and anarchy and waste and impudence in bright array having garlands on their heads, and a great company with them, hymning their praises and calling them by sweet names; insolence they term breeding, and anarchy liberty, and waste magnificence, and impudence courage. And so the young man passes out of his original nature, which was trained in the school of necessity, into the freedom and libertinism of useless and unnecessary pleasures."
refashioned:
"With great enthusiasm."

"Once they've emptied and swept clean the soul of their new convert -- who is now being initiated into what they call the 'great mysteries' -- they bring back the exiled vices in a grand procession, decked in garlands and bright array, with a chorus singing their praises and giving them flattering new names. Insolence they call 'good breeding.' Anarchy they call 'liberty.' Waste they call 'magnificence.' Impudence they call 'courage.'

And so the young man leaves behind his original nature, trained in the school of necessity, and enters the freedom and license of useless, unnecessary pleasures."
elegant: The soul is emptied and swept clean, then refilled with vices parading as virtues: insolence renamed 'good breeding,' anarchy renamed 'liberty,' waste renamed 'magnificence,' impudence renamed 'courage.' The young man abandons the school of necessity for the license of unnecessary pleasures.
summary: After purging the soul of its old restraints, the democratic appetites stage a triumphal procession of vices disguised as virtues: insolence becomes 'good breeding,' anarchy becomes 'liberty,' waste becomes 'magnificence,' and impudence becomes 'courage.' The young man passes from disciplined necessity to unrestrained pleasure.
distilled: The democratic soul replaces virtues with vices wearing flattering names -- insolence as breeding, anarchy as liberty, waste as magnificence, impudence as courage.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Great mysteries': an ironic reference to the Eleusinian Mysteries, the most sacred religious initiations in Athens -- the young man's corruption is presented as a parody of spiritual initiation. -- The four renamings form a systematic inversion: each cardinal vice is given the name of the virtue it displaces. -- 'Emptied and swept clean': echoes a passage in Matthew 12:43-45 about an unclean spirit returning to a swept house (though the Biblical parallel is chronologically later, the structural parallel is notable). -- 'School of necessity': the oligarchic training in restraint, which though imperfect, at least maintained order. -- The passage describes a complete transvaluation of values -- compare Nietzsche's use of this concept.

narrative:
"Yes," he said, "the change in him is visible enough."

"After this he lives on, spending his money and labour and time on unnecessary pleasures quite as much as on necessary ones; but if he be fortunate, and is not too much disordered in his wits, when years have elapsed, and the heyday of passion is over -- supposing that he then re-admits into the city some part of the exiled virtues, and does not wholly give himself up to their successors -- in that case he balances his pleasures and lives in a sort of equilibrium, putting the government of himself into the hands of the one which comes first and wins the turn; and when he has had enough of that, then into the hands of another; he despises none of them but encourages them all equally."
refashioned:
"Yes, the change in him is clear enough."

"After this transformation, he lives spending his money, effort, and time on unnecessary pleasures just as much as necessary ones. If he's lucky and doesn't go completely off the rails, then as the years pass and youthful passion fades, he may readmit some of the exiled virtues and not entirely surrender to their replacements. In that case, he reaches a kind of equilibrium, handing control of himself to whichever desire happens to come along first. When he tires of that one, he hands control to another. He despises none of his desires and encourages them all equally."
elegant: The democratic man divides himself equally between necessary and unnecessary pleasures. If lucky, he reaches an unstable equilibrium in middle age, handing governance to whichever desire arrives first. He refuses to rank his pleasures -- all are equal, none is despised, each takes its turn ruling.
summary: The democratic man spends equally on necessary and unnecessary pleasures. If fortunate, he reaches a kind of equilibrium in later life, handing inner governance to whichever desire happens to arrive first. He treats all pleasures as equal, refusing to privilege any over another.
distilled: The democratic man lives in unstable equilibrium, handing governance to whichever desire arrives first and treating all pleasures as equal.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Putting the government into the hands of the one which comes first': the democratic soul's governance is literally random -- rule by lot, mirroring the democratic state's use of sortition. -- 'Despises none, encourages all equally': the democratic principle of equality applied to desires -- no pleasure is judged better or worse than any other. This is the psychological equivalent of giving 'equality to equals and unequals alike' (8.39). -- The democratic man's best case is a fortunate moderation -- not principled virtue but mere balance of competing impulses. -- 'Heyday of passion': suggests that democratic excess is partly a function of youth, and maturity may bring some natural correction.

narrative:
"Very true," he said.

"Neither does he receive or let pass into the fortress any true word of advice; if any one says to him that some pleasures are the satisfactions of good and noble desires, and others of evil desires, and that he ought to use and honour some and chastise and master the others -- whenever this is repeated to him he shakes his head and says that they are all alike, and that one is as good as another."
refashioned:
"Very true."

"And he refuses to admit any true counsel into his inner fortress. If someone tells him that some pleasures come from good and noble desires while others come from base ones, and that he should cultivate the first and discipline the second -- he just shakes his head and insists that all pleasures are the same. One is as good as another."
elegant: The democratic man rejects all counsel that distinguishes between noble and base pleasures. He insists all desires are equal -- one is as good as another -- and bars the gate against any wisdom that would introduce hierarchy.
summary: The democratic man refuses any advice that distinguishes between noble and base pleasures. When told he should honor some desires and discipline others, he shakes his head and insists all pleasures are alike and equally valid.
distilled: The democratic man refuses to distinguish noble from base pleasures, insisting all desires are equal and rejecting any counsel that introduces hierarchy.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The refusal to rank pleasures is the democratic soul's defining philosophical error. For Plato, not all pleasures are equal: some are tied to genuine goods (knowledge, virtue) and others to mere appetite. See Book IX (580d-587a) for the hierarchy of pleasures. -- 'Fortress' continues the military metaphor of the soul's acropolis. -- 'Shakes his head': a vivid gesture of willful ignorance. -- This egalitarianism of desire directly contradicts the Republic's central claim that reason should rule the soul, just as philosophers should rule the state. -- The passage critiques moral relativism: if all pleasures are equal, there is no basis for virtue.

narrative:
"Yes," he said; "that is the way with him."

"Yes," I said, "he lives from day to day indulging the appetite of the hour; and sometimes he is lapped in drink and strains of the flute; then he becomes a water-drinker, and tries to get thin; then he takes a turn at gymnastics; sometimes idling and neglecting everything, then once more living the life of a philosopher; often he is busy with politics, and starts to his feet and says and does whatever comes into his head; and, if he is emulous of any one who is a warrior, off he is in that direction, or of men of business, once more in that. His life has neither law nor order; and this distracted existence he terms joy and bliss and freedom; and so he goes on."
refashioned:
"Yes, that's how he is."

"He lives day to day, indulging whatever appetite strikes him at the moment. One day he's deep in drink and music; the next he's on a water diet trying to lose weight. Then he takes up exercise. Then he's idle, neglecting everything. Then he fancies himself a philosopher. Then he's suddenly consumed by politics, jumping up and saying and doing whatever comes into his head. If he admires a soldier, he rushes off in that direction. If he admires a businessman, off he goes that way instead. His life has no law and no order -- and this scattered, aimless existence he calls joy, happiness, and freedom."
elegant: The democratic man lives from impulse to impulse: drinking, dieting, exercising, philosophizing, politicking, soldiering, doing business -- whatever strikes his fancy at the moment. His life has no law and no order. He calls this chaos joy, happiness, and freedom.
summary: The democratic man lives moment to moment, cycling through drinking, dieting, exercise, philosophy, politics, and business as each impulse strikes. His life follows no law or order, yet he calls this aimless existence joy, happiness, and freedom.
distilled: The democratic man's life is a chaotic sequence of passing impulses -- drinking, philosophizing, politicking, soldiering -- which he calls freedom and happiness.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- This is one of the Republic's most famous and psychologically acute passages. The portrait of the democratic personality type -- restless, impulsive, cycling through enthusiasms without commitment -- resonates across centuries. -- 'Strains of the flute': the aulos, associated with Dionysian ecstasy and emotional abandon. -- 'Living the life of a philosopher': philosophy becomes just another hobby, not a life commitment -- a pointed critique. -- 'Neither law nor order': anomia and ataxia -- the same charges leveled against the democratic state. -- 'This distracted existence he terms joy and bliss and freedom': the key irony -- what the democratic man calls freedom is actually enslavement to random impulse. -- Compare Kierkegaard's 'aesthetic stage' in Either/Or.

narrative:
"Yes," he replied, "he is all liberty and equality."

"Yes," I said; "his life is motley and manifold and an epitome of the lives of many; -- he answers to the State which we described as fair and spangled. And many a man and many a woman will take him for their pattern, and many a constitution and many an example of manners is contained in him."
refashioned:
"Yes," he replied, "he is the very embodiment of liberty and equality."

"His life is motley and multifaceted -- an epitome of many lives in one. He's the human counterpart of the state we called fair and spangled. Many men and women will take him as their model, and many a constitution and way of life is contained within him."
elegant: The democratic man is the embodiment of liberty and equality: a motley, manifold life that epitomizes many lives at once. He is the human counterpart of the 'fair and spangled' democratic state, and many will take him as their model.
summary: The democratic man embodies liberty and equality, living a motley, manifold life that contains many lives within it. He corresponds to the 'fair and spangled' democratic state and serves as a model for many men and women.
distilled: The democratic man is a motley epitome of many lives -- the human mirror of democracy's spangled, attractive disorder.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Motley and manifold' (poikilos kai pantodapos): the same language used to describe the democratic state as an 'embroidered robe' in 8.36. -- 'Epitome of the lives of many': the democratic personality contains all possible lifestyles within one person -- a microcosm of democratic variety. -- 'Many a man and many a woman': unusually inclusive language for Plato, reflecting democracy's extension of the model across gender. -- The democratic man is attractive precisely because of his variety -- but for Plato, variety without order is a defect, not a virtue.

narrative:
"Just so."

"Let him then be set over against democracy; he may truly be called the democratic man."

"Let that be his place," he said.

"Last of all comes the most beautiful of all, man and State alike, tyranny and the tyrant; these we have now to consider."

"Quite true," he said.

"Say then, my friend, in what manner does tyranny arise? -- that it has a democratic origin is evident."

"Clearly."

"And does not tyranny spring from democracy in the same manner as democracy from oligarchy -- I mean, after a sort?"

"How?"

"The good which oligarchy proposed to itself and the means by which it was maintained was excess of wealth -- am I not right?"

"Yes."

"And the insatiable desire of wealth and the neglect of all other things for the sake of money-getting was also the ruin of oligarchy?"

"True."

"And democracy has her own good, of which the insatiable desire brings her to dissolution?"

"What good?"

"Freedom," I replied; "which, as they tell you in a democracy, is the glory of the State -- and that therefore in a democracy alone will the freeman of nature deign to dwell."

"Yes; the saying is in everybody's mouth."

"I was going to observe, that the insatiable desire of this and the neglect of other things introduces the change in democracy, which occasions a demand for tyranny."

"How so?"

"When a democracy which is thirsting for freedom has evil cupbearers presiding over the feast, and has drunk too deeply of the strong wine of freedom, then, unless her rulers are very amenable and give a plentiful draught, she calls them to account and punishes them, and says that they are cursed oligarchs."

"Yes," he replied, "a very common occurrence."

"Yes," I said; "and loyal citizens are insultingly termed by her slaves who hug their chains and men of naught; she would have subjects who are like rulers, and rulers who are like subjects: these are men after her own heart, whom she praises and honours both in private and public. Now, in such a State, can liberty have any limit?"

"Certainly not."

"By degrees the anarchy finds a way into private houses, and ends by getting among the animals and infecting them."
refashioned:
"Just so."

"Then let him stand as the democratic man."

"Let that be his place."

"Last of all -- and most 'beautiful' of all -- comes tyranny and the tyrant, both state and man. These we must now examine."

"Quite true."

"Tell me, then: how does tyranny arise? Its democratic origin is clear enough."

"Clearly."

"And doesn't tyranny spring from democracy in the same way democracy springs from oligarchy -- through excess of its own defining good?"

"How?"

"Oligarchy's defining good was wealth, and insatiable greed for wealth was also its ruin."

"Yes."

"Democracy likewise has its own defining good, and insatiable desire for that good brings about its destruction."

"What good?"

"Freedom," I replied. "In a democracy, they tell you that freedom is the state's highest glory, and that only in a democracy can a naturally free person bear to live."

"Yes, you hear that everywhere."

"My point is that the insatiable desire for freedom, combined with the neglect of everything else, creates the conditions that demand tyranny."

"How so?"

"When a democracy thirsting for freedom gets bad cupbearers presiding over the feast and drinks the strong wine of freedom too deeply, then -- unless its leaders are extremely compliant and pour generously -- it punishes them and denounces them as cursed oligarchs."

"Yes, that happens all the time."

"And loyal citizens get insulted as 'slaves who love their chains' and 'nobodies.' Democracy wants subjects who act like rulers and rulers who act like subjects -- those are the people she honors in public and private. In such a state, can liberty have any limit?"

"Certainly not."

"Gradually the anarchy creeps into private households and eventually infects even the animals."
elegant: Each constitution is destroyed by excess of its own defining good: oligarchy by insatiable greed, democracy by insatiable freedom. Democracy drunk on freedom denounces all authority as oligarchy, honors the insubordinate, insults the loyal, and demands rulers who act like subjects. The anarchy spreads from public life into homes and eventually infects even the animals.
summary: Tyranny arises from democracy through the same mechanism that produced democracy from oligarchy: the insatiable pursuit of the regime's own defining good. Oligarchy was destroyed by excess wealth; democracy is destroyed by excess freedom. The democracy, drunk on liberty, denounces all authority, honors insubordination, and lets anarchy spread from public life into homes and even to animals.
distilled: Each regime is destroyed by excess of its own good -- oligarchy by greed, democracy by freedom -- and democracy's insatiable liberty creates the conditions for tyranny.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'Most beautiful': deeply ironic -- tyranny is the worst, not the most beautiful. -- The structural parallel between oligarchy's fall (excess wealth) and democracy's fall (excess freedom) is one of Plato's most elegant analytical moves. -- 'Evil cupbearers' and 'strong wine of freedom': the metaphor of the feast suggests democracy as Dionysian excess. -- 'Slaves who hug their chains': the democratic inversion where obedience is mocked and insubordination celebrated. -- 'Rulers who are like subjects': the collapse of natural hierarchy. -- 'Anarchy among the animals': Plato pushes the democratic leveling principle to absurdity to show where the logic leads. -- This passage is the pivot point of Book VIII: from democratic analysis to the genesis of tyranny.

narrative:
"How do you mean?"

"I mean that the father grows accustomed to descend to the level of his sons and to fear them, and the son is on a level with his father, he having no respect or reverence for either of his parents; and this is his freedom, and the metic is equal with the citizen and the citizen with the metic, and the stranger is quite as good as either."
refashioned:
"What do you mean?"

"I mean that the father gets used to lowering himself to his sons' level and starts fearing them. The son considers himself the equal of his father, showing no respect or reverence for either parent -- and this he calls freedom. The resident alien is equal to the citizen, and the citizen to the alien, and the foreigner is considered as good as either."
elegant: Democratic freedom dissolves all natural hierarchies: fathers fear their sons, sons disrespect their parents, citizens and foreigners become interchangeable. Every distinction is leveled in the name of liberty.
summary: Democratic freedom erodes all hierarchies: fathers descend to their sons' level and fear them, sons show no reverence for parents, and distinctions between citizens, resident aliens, and foreigners are eliminated -- all in the name of liberty.
distilled: Democratic freedom dissolves every natural hierarchy -- between parents and children, citizens and foreigners -- leveling all distinctions.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The father-son reversal is the domestic mirror of political anarchy. -- Metic (metoikos): a resident alien in Athens who had certain obligations but not full citizenship rights. The democratic leveling described here would dissolve the legal distinctions between citizen, metic, and xenos (foreigner). -- 'This is his freedom': the ironic identification of disrespect with liberty. -- Compare Aristotle, Politics V.9 on the democratic tendency to equalize all differences. -- The passage reflects conservative Athenian anxieties about social dissolution -- but Plato's critique is philosophical, not merely reactionary: hierarchy for him is about natural aptitude and education, not birth.

narrative:
"Yes," he said, "that is the way."

"And these are not the only evils," I said -- "there are several lesser ones: In such a state of society the master fears and flatters his scholars, and the scholars despise their masters and tutors; young and old are all alike; and the young man is on a level with the old, and is ready to compete with him in word or deed; and old men condescend to the young and are full of pleasantry and gaiety; they are loth to be thought morose and authoritative, and therefore they adopt the manners of the young."
refashioned:
"Yes, that's how it works."

"And there are more evils besides. In such a society, teachers fear and flatter their students, and students despise their teachers and tutors. Young and old become indistinguishable. Young people compete openly with their elders in speech and action. Old people, meanwhile, adopt youthful manners, full of jokes and lightheartedness -- they're terrified of being thought stern or authoritarian."
elegant: In democratic society, teachers flatter students, students despise teachers. Young and old become indistinguishable as the old adopt youthful manners to avoid seeming authoritarian. Every hierarchy of experience and wisdom is dissolved.
summary: Democratic leveling extends to education and age: teachers fear and flatter students, students despise teachers, and old people adopt youthful manners to avoid appearing stern. The hierarchy between young and old dissolves as elders compete to seem youthful and easy-going.
distilled: Democratic equality dissolves the authority of teachers over students and elders over youth, as everyone races to appear casual and non-hierarchical.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- 'The master fears and flatters his scholars': one of Plato's most frequently quoted observations, recurring in educational debates across millennia. -- The reversal of the teacher-student relationship directly undermines the Republic's educational program (Books II-III, VII). -- 'Old men condescend to the young': the elderly abandon their natural role as bearers of wisdom and authority, adopting youth culture instead. -- 'Loth to be thought morose and authoritative': the democratic fear of appearing serious or demanding. -- Compare Aristophanes' Clouds, where the corruption of education is a central theme. -- Aristotle discusses the democratic tendency toward permissiveness in Politics V.9.

narrative:
"Quite true," he said.

"The last extreme of popular liberty is when the slave bought with money, whether male or female, is just as free as his or her purchaser; nor must I forget to tell of the liberty and equality of the two sexes in relation to each other."

"Why not, as Aeschylus says, utter the word which rises to our lips?"
refashioned:
"Quite true."

"The ultimate extreme of popular liberty is when slaves -- purchased with money, whether male or female -- are just as free as their owners. And I shouldn't neglect to mention the liberty and equality of the sexes in their relation to each other."

"Why not, as Aeschylus says, speak the word that rises to our lips?"
elegant: The extreme of democratic liberty: even purchased slaves become as free as their owners, and the sexes achieve full equality. Adeimantus quotes Aeschylus to encourage the observation.
summary: Socrates identifies the ultimate extreme of democratic freedom: slaves become as free as their masters, and the sexes achieve equality. Adeimantus encourages him to speak freely, quoting Aeschylus.
distilled: Democratic liberty reaches its extreme when even slaves are as free as their owners and the sexes are fully equalized.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- The reference to slave equality would have been striking to ancient Greek audiences, for whom slavery was a foundational institution. -- 'Liberty and equality of the two sexes': Plato himself advocated equality of education and opportunity for women in Book V (451d-457b), making this passage complexly ironic -- he approves of gender equality in principle but criticizes the unphilosophical, undisciplined form it takes in democracy. -- Aeschylus: the quotation is likely from a lost play. The phrase encourages frankness (parrhesia), itself a characteristic democratic value. -- The passage reveals a tension in Plato's thought: some democratic developments (gender equality) align with his own proposals, but he objects to their anarchic, unprincipled implementation.

narrative: "That is what I am doing," I replied; "and I must add that no one who does not know would believe, how much greater is the liberty which the animals who are under the dominion of man have in a democracy than in any other State: for truly, the she-dogs, as the proverb says, are as good as their she-mistresses, and the horses and asses have a way of marching along with all the rights and dignities of freemen; and they will run at anybody who comes in their way if he does not leave the road clear for them: and all things are just ready to burst with liberty."
refashioned: "That's what I'm doing," I replied. "And I must add something that no one would believe who hadn't seen it: even the animals under human control have far more liberty in a democracy than anywhere else. As the proverb goes, the female dogs become just like their mistresses. Horses and donkeys strut down the street with all the rights and dignities of free citizens, and they'll charge at anyone who doesn't get out of their way. Everything is practically bursting with liberty."
elegant: Even the animals are freer in a democracy: dogs become like their mistresses, horses and donkeys strut with the dignity of free citizens and charge anyone in their path. Everything is bursting with liberty.
summary: Socrates extends the democratic leveling principle to its comic extreme: even animals are freer under democracy. Dogs behave like their owners, and horses and donkeys strut through the streets with the dignity of citizens, running down anyone in their way. Everything is bursting with liberty.
distilled: Democratic liberty extends even to animals -- dogs, horses, and donkeys strut like free citizens, and everything bursts with unrestrained freedom.
notes: Speakers: Socrates and Adeimantus. -- This passage is often read as comic hyperbole, but it makes a serious philosophical point: if the principle of universal equality is followed consistently, it has no natural stopping point. -- 'She-dogs as good as their she-mistresses': a proverb suggesting like master, like pet. -- The image of animals strutting with 'rights and dignities' is deliberately absurd, designed to show where the democratic logic of unlimited freedom leads. -- Plato's humor is at its sharpest here. -- Compare Aristophanes' Wasps and Birds for similar comic treatment of democratic excess.

narrative:
"When I take a country walk," he said, "I often experience what you describe. You and I have dreamed the same thing."

"And above all," I said, "and as the result of all, see how sensitive the citizens become; they chafe impatiently at the least touch of authority and at length, as you know, they cease to care even for the laws, written or unwritten; they will have no one over them."

"Yes," he said, "I know it too well."

"Such, my friend," I said, "is the fair and glorious beginning out of which springs tyranny."

"Glorious indeed," he said. "But what is the next step?"

"The ruin of oligarchy is the ruin of democracy; the same disease magnified and intensified by liberty overmasters democracy -- the truth being that the excessive increase of anything often causes a reaction in the opposite direction; and this is the case not only in the seasons and in vegetable and animal life, but above all in forms of government."

"True."

"The excess of liberty, whether in States or individuals, seems only to pass into excess of slavery."

"Yes, the natural order."

"And so tyranny naturally arises out of democracy, and the most aggravated form of tyranny and slavery out of the most extreme form of liberty?"

"As we might expect."

"That, however, was not, as I believe, your question -- you rather desired to know what is that disorder which is generated alike in oligarchy and democracy, and is the ruin of both?"

"Just so," he replied.

"Well," I said, "I meant to refer to the class of idle spendthrifts, of whom the more courageous are the leaders and the more timid the followers, the same whom we were comparing to drones, some stingless, and others having stings."

"A very just comparison."
